title: How low will it go … an updated look at forecast for ‘Market Demand’ in VIC and SA on Thursday 1st January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How low will it go … an updated look at forecast for ‘Market Demand’ in VIC and SA on Thursday 1st January 2026" posted at 31-12-2025 10:08:00 AM is: The article reports updated AEMO forecasts showing extremely low market demand in Victoria and South Australia on 1 January 2026, with Victoria potentially falling below 1,200 MW (a new record low) and South Australia dipping to -257 MW at 13:30, triggering Minimum System Load (MSL2) market notices. This is driven by a surge in residential battery installations (estimated 155,000 systems in six months) after a federal government subsidy scheme began on 1 July 2025, combined with record rooftop solar penetration, which together reduce daytime grid demand and risk system instability. The AEMO is seeking market responses and may need to intervene. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 1 January 2026 (Thursday), from 11:00 to 14:30 in SA and 12:30 to 13:30 in VIC, with potential intervention before 07:00 (SA) and 11:30 (VIC) on that day. Its root_cause is: Rapid uptake of residential battery systems (fueled by government subsidy) and high rooftop solar generation, causing demand to fall below minimum system load thresholds, threatening secure grid operation. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no physical accident, but a forecast operational risk). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-01-2026. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia (within the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: AEMO (market operator), electricity market participants (generators, retailers), residential customers with solar and batteries. Its cause type is: Policy-driven demand reduction and renewable generation surplus. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Federal government battery subsidy scheme introduced on 1 July 2025 driving a boom in residential battery installations (≈1,300 systems/day by December 2025). 2) High rooftop solar output during midday hours. 3) Both factors reduce net demand (operational demand) to record low levels, triggering MSL2 conditions.

title: Belatedly noting ‘Topical Report #2 Australia’s Progress to Net Zero by 2050’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Belatedly noting ‘Topical Report #2 Australia’s Progress to Net Zero by 2050’" posted at 30-12-2025 09:02:00 AM is: This article highlights the release of Net Zero Australia’s Topical Report #2, which warns that Australia is likely to miss its 2030 renewable energy target by up to a decade due to insufficient buildout of renewables and firming capacity. It discusses the growing challenge of meeting climate goals and the potential flow-on effects on the ideal closure schedule for coal-fired generation, referencing multiple earlier analyses that point to a gap between aspiration and reality. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium to long-term (2025-2040). Its root_cause is: Slow progress in renewable energy deployment and lack of sufficient firming capacity. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (policy report). Its dates is: 11-12-2025 (report release) and 30-12-2025 (article). Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Grid operators, energy generators, policymakers, and consumers. Its cause type is: Policy/planning gap. Its causes is: Insufficient renewable energy buildout, lack of replacement dispatchable capacity, disconnect between political targets and actual progress.

title: Forecast for coincident MSL2 on Thursday 1st January 2026 (in Victoria and South Australia)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast for coincident MSL2 on Thursday 1st January 2026 (in Victoria and South Australia)" posted at 29-12-2025 07:31:00 PM is: AEMO issued market notices on 29 December 2025 forecasting Minimum System Load (MSL) conditions for Victoria and South Australia on 1 January 2026, with Victoria projected to fall below MSL2 (minimum 959 MW, only 104 MW above MSL3) and South Australia below MSL1 (minimum -246 MW, also below MSL2), indicating an elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state due to low holiday demand and likely high solar output; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability (minimum system load conditions). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 1 January 2026 (the forecast event day). Its root_cause is: Low forecast demand during the holiday period, exacerbated by high rooftop solar generation. Its accident scale is: Forecast condition with elevated risk of AEMO intervention; no physical accident. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 01-01-2026. Its affected region is: Victoria and South Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, generators, and system operators. Its cause type is: Operational / market condition. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [1] Forecast operational demand below MSL thresholds; [2] Holiday-induced low demand (New Year's Day); [3] Likely high penetration of non-scheduled renewable generation (e.g., rooftop solar).

title: Low demand (and Actual MSL1,2) in South Australia on Friday 26th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Low demand (and Actual MSL1,2) in South Australia on Friday 26th December 2025" posted at 26-12-2025 01:24:00 PM is: This article reports that on Thursday 25th December 2025 (Christmas Day) new lowest points for Market Demand were recorded in both Victoria and South Australia, and a NEMwatch snapshot at 12:25 on Friday 26th December 2025 shows continued very low demand in South Australia, with Actual MSL1 and MSL2 levels also notably low. The event is a normal seasonal occurrence driven by public holiday reduced consumption. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (day-ahead and real-time market conditions on 25-26 December 2025). Its root_cause is: extremely low electricity demand due to the Christmas holiday period. Its accident scale is: None (no accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 25-12-2025 and 26-12-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (primary) and Victoria (mentioned). Its affected key users is: electricity generators, market operators, and grid operators. Its cause type is: normal operational (seasonal/holiday). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: public holiday on 25 December leading to reduced commercial and industrial consumption; continuation of low demand on 26 December due to the extended holiday period.

title: About directions to Blyth Battery on Thursday 25th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "About directions to Blyth Battery on Thursday 25th December 2025" posted at 26-12-2025 01:18:00 PM is: On Christmas Day 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a direction to Blyth Battery in South Australia to manage an elevated risk of Minimum System Load (MSL) due to extremely low demand. The direction required the battery to remain synchronised and follow dispatch targets from 07:00 to 16:00, with market notices documenting the intervention and its cancellation. The battery’s bidding behaviour showed preparations for the MSL event, including discharging before the direction and then following AEMO’s instructions. The event highlights AEMO’s procedures for maintaining grid stability during low-demand periods on public holidays. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 25 December 2025, short-term (same day). Its root_cause is: Extremely low electricity demand on Christmas Day leading to insufficient load to maintain a secure operating state, prompting AEMO intervention. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (no accident; controlled direction). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 25-12-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA region). Its affected key users is: Blyth Battery Pty Ltd (operator of Blyth BESS). Its cause type is: Minimum System Load risk / low demand. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Christmas Day holiday reducing electricity consumption; AEMO detecting elevated risk of insufficient demand; AEMO issuing direction to Blyth Battery to stay synchronised and manage MSL.

title: Return to service struggles at Vales Point 5
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Return to service struggles at Vales Point 5" posted at 26-12-2025 10:20:00 AM is: Vales Point 5, a coal-fired unit offline since August 2025 due to turbine vibrations, briefly returned to service on Christmas Day 2025 but remains constrained to low-load operations because of a subsequent auxiliary plant failure, indicating an ongoing struggle to fully restore capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: August 2025 to present (December 2025) and likely continuing. Its root_cause is: Turbine vibrations initially causing the long outage, compounded by auxiliary plant failure during the return-to-service attempt. Its accident scale is: Single unit (Vales Point 5). Its dates are: Outage start – 26-08-2025; first output attempt – 25-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (part of the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers in New South Wales (unknown specific users). Its cause type is: Technical failure. Its causes are: 1. Turbine vibrations leading to the August 2025 outage. 2. Auxiliary plant failure limiting post-return capability to low-load operations.

title: New lowest points for ‘Market Demand’ in both VIC and SA on Thursday 25th December 2025 (Christmas Day)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "New lowest points for ‘Market Demand’ in both VIC and SA on Thursday 25th December 2025 (Christmas Day)" posted at 25-12-2025 02:06:00 PM is: On Christmas Day 2025, both Victoria and South Australia recorded new historical lows in Market Demand, with South Australia reaching -311 MW and Victoria 1,289 MW, driven by cooler weather and high rooftop solar output. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued actual Minimum System Load (MSL) notices: MSL2 for South Australia (demand below -117 MW) and MSL1 for Victoria (demand below 1,695 MW), indicating elevated risk of insufficient demand for secure grid operation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, intraday on 25 December 2025. Its root_cause is: Cooler-than-usual summer weather in southern Australia reducing electricity consumption, combined with high rooftop solar generation leading to negative demand in South Australia. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 25-12-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users is: AEMO, grid operators, and electricity market participants. Its cause type is: Weather-driven demand reduction and high distributed generation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Cooler temperatures and snow in Tasmania reducing cooling demand. 2. High rooftop solar output on a public holiday with low industrial demand. 3. Resulting negative demand in SA and low demand in VIC triggering MSL conditions.

title: Modelling prices in a post-coal NEM: When batteries dominate the market
author: Paul Bandarian
classified_content: The summary of the news "Modelling prices in a post-coal NEM: When batteries dominate the market" posted at 24-12-2025 01:03:00 PM is: This analysis models the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) in 2040 after all coal-fired generation has exited, finding that electricity prices can be kept near current levels if solar is structurally supported and batteries become the dominant price-forming technology, with intraday price curves flattening and seasonal price patterns emerging, though scarcity events during wind droughts cause episodic price spikes. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2040 (modelled year) with transition impacts unfolding from present. Its root_cause is: Closure of coal-fired generation in the NEM by 2040. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (modelling study). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: Event date: 2040 (modelled), article date: 24-12-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers, generators, and market operators. Its cause type is: Policy-driven structural change. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Retirement of all NEM coal plants by 2040, large-scale deployment of solar, wind, and battery storage, reliance on gas for peaking and reserve capacity, and shift in price formation from fossil fuels to battery storage.

title: NEM Wrapped 2025: What you might have missed on WattClarity this year
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "NEM Wrapped 2025: What you might have missed on WattClarity this year" posted at 23-12-2025 03:55:00 PM is: This article provides a comprehensive year-end review of key events and developments in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM) during 2025, as documented on the WattClarity blog. It covers extreme weather events (heatwaves, flooding, ex-Tropical Cyclone Alfred, hailstorms, a bomb cyclone), generator and equipment failures (Callide C3 clinker incident, Bulgana wind turbine fire, Waratah BESS transformer failure, Wellington North Solar Farm grass fire), grid instability (transmission faults from dust and salt, frequency fluctuations from a data glitch), and market reactions (record demand, price spikes exceeding $15,000/MWh, tight supply conditions). Policy developments (nuclear debate, Nelson Review, Capacity Investment Scheme) and operational changes (Frequency Performance Payments) also feature. The year saw significant volatility and reliability challenges across multiple regions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of event impact on electricity_market is: Throughout 2025, with specific events from January to December. Its root_cause is: Combination of extreme weather events and generator/equipment failures. Its accident scale is: Multiple incidents affecting tens to hundreds of thousands of customers (e.g., 140,000 in NSW, 161,000 in QLD) and causing extended outages and market disruptions. Its dates is: Various dates in 2025, including Jan 15, Feb 3, Mar 8, Mar 15, Apr 28, May 26, May 28, Jun 12, Jul 1, Aug 19, Nov 7, Nov 26, Dec 6, and others. Its affected region is: NEM regions of New South Wales, Queensland, South Australia, Victoria, and others. Its affected key users is: Residential customers, commercial and industrial users, electricity market participants (generators, retailers), and grid operators. Its cause type is: Natural disasters (weather) and technical/operational failures. Its causes is: Heatwaves driving high demand; heavy rainfall flooding substation; ex-Tropical Cyclone Alfred causing widespread outages; transmission faults from dust and salt; clinker incident at Callide C3; wind turbine fire at Bulgana; grass fire at Wellington North Solar Farm; hailstorms in South East Queensland; bomb cyclone in NSW; data glitch from self-forecast software; transformer failure at Waratah BESS; prolonged cold, still, cloudy weather driving price spikes.

title: Queensland’s overnight *minimum* ‘Market Demand’ was the *highest* ever!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Queensland’s overnight *minimum* ‘Market Demand’ was the *highest* ever!" posted at 23-12-2025 03:49:00 PM is: A severe heatwave in Queensland, with apparent temperatures at Brisbane airport remaining above 27°C overnight and climbing to 30°C by early morning on 22 December 2025, drove the minimum 'Market Demand' to a record 6,748 MW at 05:00 NEM time, likely the highest overnight low since the National Electricity Market began. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (overnight of 22-23 December 2025). Its root_cause is: Sustained high overnight temperatures increasing cooling demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates is: 22-12-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland (primarily Brisbane). Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Natural (heatwave). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) High apparent temperatures persisting overnight; 2) Increased air conditioning usage; 3) Resulting in record high minimum demand.

title: The ‘Gas Market Review Report’ released on 22nd December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The ‘Gas Market Review Report’ released on 22nd December 2025" posted at 22-12-2025 11:15:00 AM is: The Australian Federal Government released its Gas Market Review Report on 22 December 2025, announcing a scheme requiring LNG exporters to reserve 15–25% of production for the domestic market, with the exact proportion to be determined after consultation. The scheme, intended to commence in 2027, aims to increase domestic supply, drive down prices, and respect existing contracts while providing long-term certainty for investment and minimising impacts on trade partners. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: fuel supply issues (gas). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 2027 onward (long-term). Its root_cause is: insufficient domestic gas supply leading to high prices and affordability concerns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 22-12-2025. Its affected region is: Australia (national scope). Its affected key users is: Australian homes, businesses (especially commercial and industrial users), gas infrastructure providers. Its cause type is: Policy/regulatory. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Government decision to mandate a gas reservation scheme for domestic market.

title: A +460MW jump in NSW demand to 13:10 on Saturday 20th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A +460MW jump in NSW demand to 13:10 on Saturday 20th December 2025" posted at 21-12-2025 04:53:00 PM is: A sudden +460MW increase in market demand occurred in New South Wales at 13:10 on 20 December 2025, coinciding with a drop in large solar output and likely rooftop PV, indicating a rapid net-demand shift due to solar variability. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: drop in solar generation. Its accident scale is: +460MW demand jump. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 20-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: renewable variability. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: drop in large solar output; possible drop in rooftop PV.

title: Frequency wandering south, on Sunday afternoon 21st December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Frequency wandering south, on Sunday afternoon 21st December 2025" posted at 21-12-2025 03:51:00 PM is: The article reports a frequency wandering event on Sunday afternoon 21 December 2025, caused by variable renewable energy (VRE)-induced frequency weakness. Though remaining within the normal operating frequency band (NOFB), the event highlights a growing pattern of instability, prompting questions about the sustainability of the Semi-Scheduled generation category and the need for additional Regulation FCAS. It references AEMO's acknowledgment of the challenge and the recently released Nelson Review. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (hours) for the specific event, but with ongoing systemic implications. Its root_cause is: High penetration of variable renewable energy (VRE) leading to insufficient system strength and regulation, exacerbated by semi-scheduled generation characteristics. Its accident scale is: Minor frequency event within normal operating band (NOFB). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 21-12-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: All grid participants, including renewable generators, AEMO, and consumers reliant on frequency stability. Its cause type is: Technical/operational - renewable integration challenge. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) High variability and low inertia from VRE; 2) Inadequate regulation FCAS procurement; 3) Structural issues with the Semi-Scheduled classification.

title: Actual lack of reserve level 2 on Friday 19th December 2025 in NSW
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Actual lack of reserve level 2 on Friday 19th December 2025 in NSW" posted at 19-12-2025 04:30:00 PM is: On 19 December 2025 at 16:11, AEMO issued an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) notice for the NSW region, effective from 16:00 to 17:00, with a minimum reserve available of 664 MW against a required 700 MW capacity reserve. The condition was triggered because actual demand persisted higher than forecast, as the forecast overestimated the cooling effect of cooler temperatures, leaving insufficient generation reserves and prompting AEMO to seek an immediate market response. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 19 December 2025, 16:00–17:00 (1 hour). Its root_cause is: Higher-than-forecasted electricity demand due to cooler temperatures not mitigating demand as expected. Its accident scale is: Regional (NSW) – LOR2 indicates a reserve shortfall of 36 MW below the required 700 MW. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 19-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users are: All electricity consumers in NSW and AEMO (market operator). Its cause type is: Operational/forecast error (demand underestimation). Its causes are: 1) Forecast demand overestimated the mitigating impact of cooler temperatures, leading to actual demand remaining higher than projected. 2) Insufficient generation or reserve capacity available to meet the unexpectedly high demand during the afternoon peak.

title: … but then the cooler change arrived in NSW, on Friday afternoon 19th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "… but then the cooler change arrived in NSW, on Friday afternoon 19th December 2025," posted at 19-12-2025 03:08:00 PM is: On Friday 19 December 2025, New South Wales (NSW) experienced a sharp drop in temperature around Sydney during the afternoon, which abruptly halted the previously rising electricity market demand that had exceeded 12,000 MW around 13:45. The cooler change relieved heat-driven consumption, causing demand growth to abate by 14:55.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: Extreme weather.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, within the same afternoon (hours).; Its root_cause is: A cooler change (temperature drop) in and around Sydney.; Its accident scale is: Unknown.; Its dates is: 19-12-2025.; Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia.; Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in NSW (not further specified).; Its cause type is: Weather/natural.; Its causes is: 1. Sustained hot weather prior to the afternoon driving high demand; 2. A sudden cooler change reducing ambient temperature; 3. Consequent reduction in demand growth.

title: NSW ‘Market Demand’ past 12,000MW … how high will it go (on Friday 19th December 2025)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "NSW ‘Market Demand’ past 12,000MW … how high will it go (on Friday 19th December 2025)?" posted at 19-12-2025 01:53:00 PM is: The article reports that New South Wales (NSW) market demand exceeded 12,000 MW at 13:45 on 19 December 2025, surpassing earlier forecasts and continuing to climb, with comparisons to a similar demand event in Queensland in January 2024; the situation indicates a likely heatwave-driven surge. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day, ongoing). Its root_cause is: High ambient temperatures increasing cooling load. Its accident scale is: N/A (not an accident). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 19-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Weather-driven demand. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Heatwave conditions leading to widespread air-conditioning use; Forecast convergence showing demand outpacing predictions.

title: Was cloud cover the driver of the steep increase in ‘Market Demand’ at lunchtime in NSW on Friday 19th December 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Was cloud cover the driver of the steep increase in ‘Market Demand’ at lunchtime in NSW on Friday 19th December 2025?" posted at 19-12-2025 12:57:00 PM is: Unforeseen cloud cover is identified as a potential trigger for a steep increase in Market Demand in NSW during lunchtime on 19 December 2025, causing earlier-than-expected volatility as solar generation dropped and net demand rose sharply after 11:30 NEM time. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes to hours). Its root_cause is: unforeseen cloud cover reducing solar generation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 19-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in NSW (including households and businesses). Its cause type is: Weather (cloud cover). Its causes is: Unforeseen cloud cover, reduced solar output, increased net demand on the grid.

title: Lunchtime volatility in NSW on Friday 19th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Lunchtime volatility in NSW on Friday 19th December 2025" posted at 19-12-2025 12:48:00 PM is: On Friday 19 December 2025, New South Wales experienced a sharp midday price spike above $1,000/MWh, driven by a steep escalation in market demand from the 11:30 dispatch interval. Interconnector flows were mostly stable, except for imports from Victoria into South Australia, indicating that the price surge was primarily a local demand-driven event rather than a supply or grid failure. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, with price volatility lasting for the lunchtime dispatch intervals on 19 December 2025. Its root_cause is: High market demand leading to a price spike above $1,000/MWh. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates is: 19-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely wholesale electricity buyers and end consumers in NSW). Its cause type is: Demand-driven. Its causes is: Steep increase in market demand.

title: Spot price elevated already Friday morning 19th December 2025 in a very hot NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spot price elevated already Friday morning 19th December 2025 in a very hot NSW" posted at 19-12-2025 11:05:00 AM is: A quick NEMwatch snapshot at 10:55 AM on 19 December 2025 shows elevated spot electricity prices in New South Wales due to extreme heat conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (same day, morning of 19 December 2025). Its root_cause is: Extreme heat in NSW driving higher demand and likely supply constraints. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 19-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Weather-related (heatwave). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [High temperatures leading to increased electricity demand, Potential generation limitations or transmission constraints due to heat].

title: Violation of the ‘N>MPWW_94T_71’ constraint equation, on Friday 19th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Violation of the ‘N>MPWW_94T_71’ constraint equation, on Friday 19th December 2025" posted at 19-12-2025 10:00:00 AM is: A constraint equation 'N>MPWW_94T_71' was violated during the 09:55 dispatch interval on Friday 19th December 2025 in the National Electricity Market (NEM). This constraint is part of the 'N-MPWW_ONE' constraint set, which the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) had slated to be invoked until 15:00 that day. Additionally, a forecast violation of the 'N>NIL_998_18' constraint equation was also observed. The violations indicate ongoing network security issues requiring operational intervention. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the day, 19 December 2025, 09:55 to 15:00). Its root_cause is: Unknown (likely enforcement of network constraints by AEMO to manage system security). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 19-12-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational / Network constraint violation. Its causes is: Violation of constraint equation N>MPWW_94T_71; forecast violation of constraint equation N>NIL_998_18.

title: Bayswater unit 2 returning to service, albeit later than expected, on Friday 19th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Bayswater unit 2 returning to service, albeit later than expected, on Friday 19th December 2025" posted at 19-12-2025 08:20:00 AM is: Bayswater Unit 2 in New South Wales is returning to service on the morning of 19 December 2025, later than originally expected. This means the unit was unavailable during the evening volatility on 18 December 2025, but is expected to be fully online by the evening of 19 December to help manage forecast volatility. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The unit's absence affected the market on 18 December 2025, and its return will affect the market on 19 December 2025. Its root_cause is: Unknown (the article does not specify why the unit was offline or why it returned later than expected). Its accident scale is: Single unit outage and return at Bayswater power station. Its dates is: 19-12-2025 (event date same as news post date). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely wholesale electricity market participants). Its cause type is: Unavailability (outage) of a coal-fired generator. Its causes is: 1) Unit 2 at Bayswater was offline prior to 19 December 2025. 2) Its return to service was delayed beyond the initially expected time.

title: Looking ahead to Friday afternoon/evening 19th December 2025 in NSW
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Looking ahead to Friday afternoon/evening 19th December 2025 in NSW" posted at 19-12-2025 08:01:00 AM is: The article recaps Thursday evening's moderate price volatility and tight supply-demand balance, then highlights forecast LOR2 conditions for Friday afternoon/evening and Sunday 21st December 2025 in NSW, indicating potential system security risks due to a tight supply-demand balance. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Friday afternoon/evening 19th December 2025 and Sunday 21st December 2025. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates are: 19-12-2025 and 21-12-2025. Its affected region is: NSW. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Unknown.

title: That’s a wrap, on Thursday evening 18th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "That’s a wrap, on Thursday evening 18th December 2025" posted at 18-12-2025 07:39:00 PM is: The NEMwatch snapshot at 19:35 on 18 December 2025 reports no load shedding (LOR3) but a LOR2 condition (lack of reserve level 2) until 19:15, indicating tight supply-demand balance. Reserve Trader negotiations began but no contracts were made or dispatched. Some price volatility occurred but was less extreme than earlier forecasts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 18 December 2025, evening hours. Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Unknown. Its causes is: Unknown.

title: The violation of the ‘N>NIL_998_18’ constraint equation
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The violation of the ‘N>NIL_998_18’ constraint equation" posted at 18-12-2025 07:17:00 PM is: A forecast-violation of the 'N>NIL_998_18' constraint equation occurred at the 19:10 dispatch interval on 18 December 2025, as previously anticipated in an earlier article. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: Forecast violation of a system normal constraint equation. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: All NEM participants and system operators. Its cause type is: Operational constraint violation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Forecast violation of the constraint equation 'N>NIL_998_18', possibly due to generation or transmission constraints].

title: About the delay in expected return to service of Vales Point 5
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "About the delay in expected return to service of Vales Point 5" posted at 18-12-2025 07:16:00 PM is: Vales Point 5, a coal-fired unit in New South Wales, has been in an unplanned outage since August 2025 due to turbine vibrations. The expected return-to-service has been repeatedly delayed, most recently on 8 December 2025, with the current estimate pushed to Tuesday evening, 23 December 2025, exacerbating supply tightness in the NSW region.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures.  
Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from August 2025 until at least 23 December 2025.  
Its root_cause is: turbine vibrations.  
Its accident scale is: single unit outage (capacity not specified).  
Its dates are: outage start 2025-08-26; expected return 2025-12-23; news date 18-12-2025.  
Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia.  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely NSW electricity consumers and market participants).  
Its cause type is: unplanned equipment failure.  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: turbine vibrations leading to forced outage on 2025-08-26; subsequent repair delays causing multiple rescheduling of return-to-service.

title: Evening volatility underway in NSW on Thursday 18th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Evening volatility underway in NSW on Thursday 18th December 2025" posted at 18-12-2025 06:29:00 PM is: On Thursday evening 18 December 2025, the New South Wales (NSW) electricity market experienced significant price volatility as a forecast tight supply-demand balance materialized, with spot prices spiking to approximately $14,000/MWh (part-way to the Market Price Cap) and market demand reaching 32,218 MW NEM-wide—slightly higher than earlier forecasts. The volatility is driven by oscillating demand levels and the rapid decline of solar generation approaching sunset, compounded by ongoing Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) negotiations to secure additional capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (evening of 18 December 2025, lasting several hours). Its root_cause is: Tight supply-demand balance due to higher-than-forecast market demand and the rapid decline of solar generation at sunset, with insufficient dispatchable capacity to meet the evening peak. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no physical accident reported; event is market volatility). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date): 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales) within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: Electricity consumers, market participants (generators, retailers), and the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO). Its cause type is: Operational / Market imbalance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Higher-than-expected market demand (32,218 MW NEM-wide); 2) Rapid decrease in solar generation as sunset approaches; 3) Delays or insufficient capacity from RERT negotiations to cover the shortfall; 4) Oscillating demand levels causing price see-saws.

title: AEMO signals intention to negotiate RERT contracts as evening supply–demand tightens in NSW on Thursday 18th December 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO signals intention to negotiate RERT contracts as evening supply–demand tightens in NSW on Thursday 18th December 2025" posted at 18-12-2025 04:59:00 PM is: AEMO published a market notice indicating its intention to commence negotiations under the Reliability and Emergency Reserve Trader (RERT) mechanism for the NSW region, covering the evening operating window from 4:00pm to 11:00pm on 18 December 2025, due to tight supply–demand conditions, to maintain system reliability if additional reserve is required. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (evening of 18 December 2025, 16:00 to 23:00 NEM time). Its root_cause is: Unknown (tight supply–demand conditions, cause not specified in the article). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: NSW (NSW1 region) in the Australian National Electricity Market. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers in NSW, but not specified). Its cause type is: Operational / supply-demand imbalance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: No specific cause events listed; AEMO is acting preemptively due to forecast tight supply–demand.

title: ‘Actual LOR1’ in NSW from 15:43 on Thursday 18th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Actual LOR1 in NSW from 15:43 on Thursday 18th December 2025" posted at 18-12-2025 03:51:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice (MN131786) declaring an Actual Lack of Reserve Level 1 (LOR1) for the NSW region from 15:30 to 21:00 on 18 December 2025, with a capacity reserve requirement of 1360 MW but only 777 MW available, indicating a significant reserve shortfall and potential grid reliability risk. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 18/12/2025 15:30 to 18/12/2025 21:00 (NEM time). Its root_cause is: Insufficient reserve capacity relative to the required margin (777 MW available vs 1360 MW required). Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported; reserve deficiency is system-wide). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 18/12/2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: NEM participants, electricity consumers in NSW, and system operators. Its cause type is: Reserve shortfall. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unknown specific cause; the deficiency likely stems from a combination of high demand, generation outages, or reduced interconnector flows, but no explicit cause is provided in the notice.

title: Forecast violation for 3 x System Normal constraint equations on Thursday evening 18th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast violation for 3 x System Normal constraint equations on Thursday evening 18th December 2025" posted at 18-12-2025 03:14:00 PM is: A short note at the 15:00 dispatch interval on Thursday 18th December 2025 highlights that in the unfiltered Constraint Equations widget, a large number of constraint equations are forecast to bind with large Marginal Values around the time of forecast volatility that evening, with three specific System Normal constraint equations (named 'N>NIL_998_18', 'N>NIL_9R6_9R5_N', and 'N>NIL_9R5_9R6_N') forecast to violate starting from the 19:00 half-hour, indicating potential grid instability and price impacts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Thursday evening (18 December 2025) from 19:00 half-hour onwards. Its root_cause is: Forecast violations of system normal constraint equations due to high binding conditions and large marginal values. Its accident scale is: Unknown (multiple constraint equations bound, three specific violations, but no explicit scale provided). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: Unknown (likely the National Electricity Market, NEM, but not specified). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Forecast violation of constraint equations. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) A large number of constraint equations forecast to bind with large Marginal Values around the evening volatility; (2) Three specific System Normal constraint equations violating from 19:00 half-hour.

title: The ‘#NSW1_E_20251218’ constraint equation, on Thursday 18th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The ‘#NSW1_E_20251218’ constraint equation, on Thursday 18th December 2025" posted at 18-12-2025 01:07:00 PM is: On 18 December 2025 at 13:00 NEM time, the constraint equation '#NSW1_E_20251218' was invoked in the National Electricity Market, as indicated by a Constraint Dashboard snapshot. No further details were provided beyond the invocation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, from 13:00 on 18 December 2025 until the constraint is lifted (unknown duration). Its root_cause is: Unknown. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Market participants in NSW, particularly generators and loads subject to the constraint. Its cause type is: Technical/Operational. Its causes is: Unknown specific cause.

title: AEMO propose rule change to reform compensation frameworks
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO propose rule change to reform compensation frameworks" posted at 18-12-2025 12:00:00 PM is: The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) has proposed a rule change to consolidate the National Electricity Market's multiple compensation schemes into a single framework, aiming to reduce uncertainty and administrative burden stemming from lessons learned during the June 2022 market suspension, where overlapping schemes led to $148.8 million in compensation payments. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future pending implementation (no timeline announced). Its root_cause is: Lessons from the June 2022 energy crisis and the resulting overlapping compensation schemes that created uncertainty for participants. Its accident scale is: N/A. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Late November 2025 (submission date). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants (generators, retailers, and other stakeholders). Its cause type is: Regulatory reform / compensation framework. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Overlapping compensation schemes during June 2022 market suspension; need to streamline and align frameworks; energy transition leading to new types of directions (e.g., to battery energy storage systems).

title: ‘Sometimes diversity does not help’ … such as on Friday 31st October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: :The summary of the news “'Sometimes diversity does not help' … such as on Friday 31st October 2025” posted at 18-12-2025 10:17:00 AM is: On 31 October 2025, the National Electricity Market (NEM) experienced a frequency drop to 49.841 Hz, outside the normal operating band, due to large aggregate dispatch errors from solar farms in Queensland and New South Wales (total +755 MW) and a smaller error from wind (+130 MW). The AEMO later explained that the errors stemmed from cloud cover forecasting inaccuracies: in NSW a thick cloud system’s timing was slightly off, while in Queensland clouds formed unexpectedly. The frequency event began at ~13:46 NEM time, reached its lowest point at 13:55:28.1, and recovered by ~14:10, lasting five dispatch intervals (about 15 minutes).; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The event occurred on 31 October 2025 from approximately 13:46 to 14:10 NEM time, with frequency fluctuations lasting five 5-minute dispatch intervals; Its root_cause is: Cloud cover forecasting errors leading to large aggregate dispatch errors in solar and wind generation; Its accident scale is: Frequency drop to 49.841 Hz (outside Normal Operating Frequency Band), lasting approximately 15 minutes; Its dates (if different) are: Event date: 31-10-2025; Article date: 18-12-2025; AEMO forum date: 03-12-2025; Its affected region is: New South Wales and Queensland (solar farms) and the broader NEM grid; Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: Operational/forecasting error; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) In NSW, a thick cloud system was observed moving east, but the forecast timing of its encroachment on solar farms was slightly off, causing the dispatch error; 2) In Queensland, clouds formed 'out of the blue', which was difficult to predict, also causing a dispatch error.

title: The ‘forecast LOR3’ that persisted through the night slips back to ‘forecast LOR2’ … but might rise again
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The ‘forecast LOR3’ that persisted through the night slips back to ‘forecast LOR2’ … but might rise again" posted at 18-12-2025 09:01:00 AM is: On Thursday 18th December 2025, a forecast LOR3 (load shedding) condition for the evening half-hours (19:00 and 19:30) in the NEM was downgraded to a sharp forecast LOR2 after the 08:00 update, but remains at risk of tightening again due to multiple coal unit outages (4 of 12 offline, another experiencing issues) and declining forecast aggregate wind generation. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures, grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (evening of 18th December 2025). Its root_cause is: Combination of coal unit outages and declining wind generation forecast. Its accident scale is: Partial generation loss (4 coal units offline, 1 with issues). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (Australian National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: General electricity consumers (potential load shedding). Its cause type is: Technical failures and weather-dependent generation decline. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) 4 of 12 coal units offline, 2) Another coal unit experiencing issues, 3) Forecast aggregate UIGF for wind declining.

title: How much Semi-Scheduled VRE expected to be available on Thursday evening 18th December 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How much Semi-Scheduled VRE expected to be available on Thursday evening 18th December 2025?" posted at 18-12-2025 06:49:00 AM is: The article highlights a forecasted LOR3 (load shedding) event on the evening of 18 December 2025 in NSW, driven by a combination of 4 out of 12 coal units being offline (with another experiencing issues) and low expected output from semi-scheduled variable renewable energy (VRE)—specifically, a steep decline in large solar at sunset and a modest, declining wind forecast during the LOR2/LOR3 period, with additional risks from late afternoon cloud cover and network congestion. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability (forecast LOR3 load shedding) and turbine/generator failures (coal unit outages). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate (evening of 18 December 2025). Its root_cause is: combination of multiple coal unit outages and low VRE availability during peak demand. Its accident scale is: LOR3 (load shedding) event with multiple coal unit failures. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW). Its affected key users is: electricity consumers (potential load shedding). Its cause type is: multiple (generation outages and weather-dependent renewable variability). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 4 coal units offline, another experiencing issues; forecast low wind output and solar decline at sunset; potential cloud cover and network congestion.

title: How many coal units in NSW expected to be available on Thursday evening 18th December 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How many coal units in NSW expected to be available on Thursday evening 18th December 2025?" posted at 18-12-2025 06:34:00 AM is: A forecast of LOR3 (load shedding) in NSW on the evening of 18 December 2025 is driven by multiple coal unit outages, with only 7 of 12 units expected available. Notably, Vales Point 5 remains offline since August 2025 due to turbine vibrations, while Bayswater 2 (unplanned) and Bayswater 4 (planned extended) are delayed, Eraring 4 is offline from an unplanned outage, and Mt Piper 2 has added an unplanned outage for the Christmas period. No major transmission line constraints are reported. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (evening of 18 December 2025) with some outages (e.g., VP5) extending over months. Its root_cause is: Multiple planned and unplanned outages across 5 NSW coal units, with the specific trigger for Vales Point 5 being turbine vibrations. Its accident scale is: Significant – 5 out of 12 NSW coal units affected, leading to a forecast of load shedding. Its dates is: 18-12-2025 (main event); outage start for Vales Point 5 was 2025-08-26. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in NSW facing potential load shedding. Its cause type is: Combination of planned maintenance extensions (Bayswater 4, Mt Piper 2) and unplanned failures (Bayswater 2, Eraring 4, Vales Point 5). Its causes is: Bayswater 2 unplanned outage not yet returned; Bayswater 4 planned outage extended past expected return; Eraring 4 recent unplanned outage; Mt Piper 2 added unplanned outage for Christmas period; Vales Point 5 extended unplanned outage due to turbine vibrations.

title: Forecast tight supply-demand balance this evening upgraded to forecast LOR3 (i.e. load shedding) in NSW, Thursday 18th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast tight supply-demand balance this evening upgraded to forecast LOR3 (i.e. load shedding) in NSW, Thursday 18th December 2025" posted at 18-12-2025 06:01:00 AM is: A blast of hot weather in Australia has escalated supply-demand concerns for the evening of 18 December 2025, with New South Wales (NSW) now facing a forecast LOR3 (load shedding) situation as market demand across the National Electricity Market (NEM) climbed above 32,000 MW; meanwhile, a previously forecast LOR2 for South Australia was downgraded and then removed. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (this evening of 18 December 2025). Its root_cause is: Extreme heat driving high electricity demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown (forecast LOR3 indicates potential load shedding but no actual outage reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in NSW (potential load shedding). Its cause type is: Weather-related. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Heatwave conditions leading to elevated market demand].

title: A quick look for updates about Waratah BESS, on Wednesday 17th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look for updates about Waratah BESS, on Wednesday 17th December 2025" posted at 17-12-2025 04:08:00 PM is: This article provides a status update on the Waratah BESS as of 17 December 2025, nearly four weeks after a long unplanned outage began on 21 November 2025. Short-term availability (ST PASA) has returned to 350MW after a planned outage ended on 2 December, but medium-term availability (MT PASA) remains at 0MW until 1 May 2026 due to an AER requirement, then steps to 350MW until October 2026, and finally to 150MW market-exposed capacity after full 850MW installation. No further official news from Akaysha Energy is noted, and the battery's constraints are linked to transformer repairs and regulatory conditions. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 21 November 2025, with partial recovery on 2 December 2025 (350MW available), but full capacity unavailable until after May 2026, and further limitations extending to October 2026 and beyond. Its root_cause is: Unknown from the article; implied transformer failure or grid connection issue requiring upgrades. Its accident scale is: Partial outage affecting up to 500MW of capacity (850MW installed vs 350MW available), with full capacity unavailable for an extended period. Its dates is: 17 December 2025 (news date); outage started 21 November 2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) and electricity consumers in NSW, particularly those reliant on the System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS). Its cause type is: Technical failure (transformer or equipment failure). Its causes is: 1. Long unplanned outage beginning 21 November 2025; 2. Subsequent partial return to service on 2 December 2025 at reduced capacity; 3. Continued unavailability of full capacity due to transformer repairs and AER requirements; 4. Projected full capacity restoration not until after May 2026.

title: Blast of hot weather drives ‘forecast LOR2’ in NSW and SA on Thursday 18th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Blast of hot weather drives ‘forecast LOR2’ in NSW and SA on Thursday 18th December 2025" posted at 17-12-2025 02:03:00 PM is: AEMO issued a NEM Local Temperature Alert on 17 December 2025, forecasting extreme heat across NSW, SA, QLD, and VIC from 17 to 21 December 2025. Specifically for Thursday 18 December, forecast temperatures reaching 39°C or higher are driving projected market demand toward 32,000 MW (the highest since July 2025) and triggering forecasts of Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) in both NSW and South Australia, indicating elevated risk of supply shortfalls. The alert requests market participants to update generator capacity offers based on local temperature impacts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate for 18 December 2025, with lingering effects through 21 December 2025. Its root_cause is: Extreme heatwave leading to increased electricity demand and potential generator deratings due to temperature limits. Its accident scale is: Unknown (forecast LOR2, not an actual outage). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 17-12-2025 (alert issue date); event impact date 18-12-2025. Its affected region is: NSW and SA (primarily), with QLD and VIC also under alerts. Its affected key users is: Market participants (generators, MNSP operators) and electricity consumers in the NEM. Its cause type is: Weather-related (extreme heat). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Forecast 39°C+ temperatures at multiple weather stations in NSW and SA; AEMO’s NEM Local Temperature Alerts triggered; high demand projections nearing record summer levels; LOR2 declarations for NSW and SA due to insufficient reserve margins.

title: A short first article, about the ‘N^^N_NIL_WGLT’ constraint equation
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A short first article, about the ‘N^^N_NIL_WGLT’ constraint equation" posted at 17-12-2025 01:18:00 PM is: AEMO implemented a new system normal constraint equation N^^N_NIL_WGLT on 8 December 2025 to manage voltage collapse risk on the Lower Tumut to Wagga 330kV line in southern NSW. The constraint has been binding frequently, with highest marginal values on 14-15 December, affecting generators in southern NSW and interconnectors Murraylink and VNI. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 8 December 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: risk of voltage collapse post-contingency trip of the Lower Tumut to Wagga 330kV line (line 051). Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 8 December 2025. Its affected region is: southern New South Wales (Wagga to Lower Tumut area). Its affected key users is: generators in southern NSW, Murraylink interconnector, and VNI interconnector. Its cause type is: Grid/system instability. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) voltage stability limit in south-west NSW for loss of Lower Tumut to Wagga 330kV line; 2) potential voltage collapse after contingency trip of line 051.

title: CSIRO releases draft of ‘2025-26 GenCost Report’ for consultation
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "CSIRO releases draft of ‘2025-26 GenCost Report’ for consultation" posted at 17-12-2025 10:34:00 AM is: The CSIRO released its draft '2025-26 GenCost Report' for public consultation on 17 December 2025, introducing a new System Levelised Cost of Electricity (SLCOE) method that estimates the mix of generation sources and transmission costs for achieving Australia's renewable energy targets. The report indicates that wind, solar, and batteries remain the cheapest options, while nuclear and offshore wind are more costly by 2050, and coal and gas costs are rising. Media outlets have covered the report's implications for energy policy and investment. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: fuel prices (coal, gas). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (to 2030 and 2050). Its root_cause is: Release of GenCost report with updated cost projections. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 17-12-2025. Its affected region is: Australia. Its affected key users is: Electricity generators, policymakers, investors, and consumers. Its cause type is: Report/Policy release. Its causes is: CSIRO releases draft GenCost report; introduces new SLCOE method; media coverage.

title: Runway cost allocation – ready for liftoff?
author: Allan O'Neil
classified_content: The summary of the news "Runway cost allocation – ready for liftoff?" posted at 15-12-2025 11:11:00 AM is: This article discusses the Australian Energy Market Commission's consultation on a rule change proposal to modify cost recovery for Contingency Frequency Control Ancillary Services (CFCAS) in the NEM, shifting from a proportional allocation to a "runway cost allocation" methodology, which would assign a larger share of costs to the largest generators and loads that drive the volume of CFCAS procurement, while reducing costs for smaller participants. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Future, pending regulatory decision. Its root_cause is: Inequitable cost allocation for CFCAS under the current proportional method. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 15-12-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Generators and market customers (loads). Its cause type is: Regulatory proposal. Its causes is: Rule change proposals submitted by Mitch O'Neill of Grids; current cost allocation method deemed unfair; proposal to implement runway cost allocation.

title: ‘N-MNYS_5_WG_CLOSE’ constraint set invoked, because of unplanned outage of Yass – Marulan 5 330kV line
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "‘N-MNYS_5_WG_CLOSE’ constraint set invoked, because of unplanned outage of Yass – Marulan 5 330kV line" posted at 10-12-2025 07:26:00 PM is: On 10 December 2025, an unplanned outage of the Yass–Marulan 5 330kV line in NSW prompted AEMO to invoke the 'N-MNYS_5_WG_CLOSE' constraint set, affecting interconnectors VIC1-NSW1 and V-S-MNSP1. The constraint set is expected to remain in effect until 14 December 2025, potentially influencing electricity prices in the region by limiting transfer capacity between Victoria and New South Wales. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 10 December 2025 to 14 December 2025. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of Yass – Marulan 5 330kV line. Its accident scale is: Single 330kV line outage. Its dates is: 10-12-2025. Its affected region is: NSW region, impacting interconnectors to Victoria. Its affected key users is: AEMO, NEM participants, generators and consumers relying on interconnector flows. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (equipment failure). Its causes is: Unplanned outage of Yass – Marulan 5 330kV line.

title: Yo-yo-ing of ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia – on Wednesday 10th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Yo-yo-ing of ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia – on Wednesday 10th December 2025" posted at 10-12-2025 05:57:00 PM is: On Wednesday 10 December 2025, South Australia experienced two large, rapid swings in market demand (a +211 MW change at 11:30 and a +232 MW change at 17:05 NEM time), driven by varying cloud cover that affected both rooftop PV and semi-scheduled solar generation. These demand gyrations also coincided with a weakness in mainland frequency, highlighting growing grid stability challenges amid the energy transition, as noted in the context of the AEMO’s 2026 ISP draft and the upcoming Nelson Review recommendations. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (day of event), with ongoing trend of increased frequency of such large demand changes in 2025. Its root_cause is: Cloud cover variability causing rapid fluctuations in solar PV output, which significantly altered net demand. Its accident scale is: Large demand swings of 211 MW and 232 MW, notable relative to the size of the South Australian region. Its dates is: 10-12-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers and market participants in South Australia, and potentially the National Electricity Market (NEM) as a whole due to frequency impacts. Its cause type is: Weather/operational (cloud cover). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Cloud cover variation; rapid changes in rooftop PV generation; changes in semi-scheduled solar farm output; consequential grid frequency weakness.

title: Another example of (presumably VRE-induced) weakness in mainland frequency – Wednesday afternoon 10th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Another example of (presumably VRE-induced) weakness in mainland frequency – Wednesday afternoon 10th December 2025" posted at 10-12-2025 05:50:00 PM is: The article describes a frequency weakness event in the mainland National Electricity Market on the afternoon of Wednesday 10 December 2025, where high-speed frequency readings from Brisbane showed a decline starting around the 16:50 dispatch interval, reaching a low of 49.884 Hz at 17:00:04.5, attributed to variable renewable energy (VRE) induced instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (most relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term, during the afternoon of 10 December 2025, with immediate but temporary frequency deviation. Its root_cause is: high penetration of variable renewable energy leading to insufficient system inertia or frequency response. Its accident scale is: minor (frequency remained within the Normal Operating Frequency Band but close to the lower edge). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-12-2025. Its affected region is: mainland NEM (Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, South Australia). Its affected key users is: all grid-connected consumers in the affected region. Its cause type is: technical/operational (VRE-induced). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: rapid decline in frequency due to variable renewable energy output variability during late afternoon ramp, insufficient system inertia to maintain frequency stability.

title: Victorian Auditor-General’s report ‘Managing the Transition to Renewable Energy’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Victorian Auditor-General’s report ‘Managing the Transition to Renewable Energy’" posted at 10-12-2025 08:53:00 AM is: The article reviews the Victorian Auditor-General’s report on the state’s renewable energy transition, focusing on the impending closure of the Yallourn coal-fired power plant. While some media outlets sensationalize the risk of blackouts and chaos, the report itself provides a balanced assessment of the challenges in maintaining grid reliability while phasing out coal. The transition is expected to affect electricity market stability and prices as Victoria moves toward renewable generation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium- to long-term (from 2025 until Yallourn’s planned closure and beyond). Its root_cause is: The mandated closure of the Yallourn coal plant and the broader policy-driven shift from coal-fired generation to renewable energy sources to meet emissions reduction targets. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported). Its dates is: 2025-12-03 (report publication date). Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: Residential and commercial electricity consumers in Victoria. Its cause type is: Policy/Regulatory. Its causes is: Closure of Yallourn coal power plant; implementation of renewable energy transition policies; grid reliability concerns during the phase-out.

title: The long-term trend of forecast errors for daily peak demand in QLD over the past seven summers
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "The long-term trend of forecast errors for daily peak demand in QLD over the past seven summers" posted at 09-12-2025 03:50:00 PM is: This article analyzes AEMO's 3-hour-ahead (P30) market demand forecast errors for daily peaks in Queensland over seven summers (2018–19 to 2024–25), revealing that forecast inaccuracy grows with higher demand peaks, particularly in recent years, due to increasing behind-the-meter rooftop PV (from ~1.5 GW to ~5 GW) masking air-conditioning load and making forecasts acutely sensitive to unexpected cloud movements, resulting in persistent under-forecasting and wider error distributions at high demand levels, especially during hot, humid summer days. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with historical data from summers 2018–19 to 2024–25, as of the publication date 09-12-2025. Its root_cause is: Growth of rooftop PV penetration (from ~1.5 GW to ~5 GW) combined with hot, humid Queensland summers and cloud movements, causing limited visibility of behind-the-meter generation and leading to short-term demand forecast errors that cascade into pricing and operational surprises. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 09-12-2025 (publication date; no distinct event date). Its affected region is: Queensland (QLD), Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator) and electricity market participants in Queensland. Its cause type is: Operational and environmental (interaction between behind-the-meter generation growth and weather variability). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Rapid increase in rooftop PV capacity from ~1.5 GW (2018) to ~5 GW (2025). 2. Hot and humid summer days driving high air-conditioning load. 3. Cloud movements causing sudden drops in rooftop PV output. 4. Limited visibility of behind-the-meter generation by AEMO. 5. Forecast models calibrated to historical patterns, not capturing duck curve changes.

title: Wellington North Solar Farm back to 94 ELAV compared to 104 in total
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-12-06 Wellington North Solar Farm outage after fire
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wellington North Solar Farm back to 94 ELAV compared to 104 in total" posted at 09-12-2025 08:42:00 AM is: A grassfire on 6 December 2025 at the 400 MW Wellington North Solar Farm in NSW temporarily suspended production; by 8–9 December the farm had returned to approximately 90% of its pre‑fire capacity, as measured by ELAV (94 of 104 elements available) and output (298 MW vs. 330 MW peak). Damage was limited to the north‑east corner with no major equipment harm expected, and the operator (Lightsource BP) anticipates full operation shortly. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short‑term (days to weeks, with ongoing partial recovery). Its root_cause is: extreme weather (thunderstorms and heatwave conditions) likely ignited a grassfire at the solar farm site. Its accident scale is: moderate – 400 MW facility, ~10% capacity reduction, damage confined to a small area; no major equipment loss. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: event date 06-12-2025; news date 09-12-2025. Its affected region is: Wellington, New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: natural disaster / external fire. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Thunderstorms and heatwave conditions on 6 December 2025 in NSW; 2) Grassfire at the solar farm site; 3) Temporary shutdown and subsequent reduced output while assessments and repairs are ongoing.

title: Early morning price volatility in South Australia on Monday 8th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Early morning price volatility in South Australia on Monday 8th December 2025" posted at 08-12-2025 11:25:00 AM is: On Monday 8th December 2025, South Australia experienced early morning price spikes between 05:00 and 05:30 (NEM time), driven by a combination of low wind generation (instantaneous capacity factor ~5%), depleted battery state of charge from around 02:00 (with declining discharge volumes and higher bid prices), and no solar farm output before sunrise. This forced the market to rely on expensive gas-fired and liquid-fuelled units, leading to significant price volatility. The episode was noted internationally as an example of supply challenges from renewable intermittency and battery limitations. Its **relevance level** is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its **category** is: market reactions. Its **Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market** is: 05:00 to 05:30 on 8th December 2025. Its **root_cause** is: insufficient dispatchable generation due to low wind output and exhausted battery storage. Its **accident scale** is: Unknown. Its **dates** (if different from the main date) is: 08-12-2025. Its **affected region** is: South Australia. Its **affected key users** are: electricity consumers and market participants facing high prices. Its **cause type** is: supply shortage from renewable intermittency and battery depletion. Its **causes** (a summarized list of cause events) are: low wind farm output (capacity factor ~5%), battery state of charge depleted by ~02:00, solar farms not yet generating, gas and liquid units bid at higher prices.

title: Wellington North Solar Farm back online (late Sunday afternoon 7th December and) Monday 8th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-12-06 Wellington North Solar Farm outage after fire
classified_content: The summary of the news "Wellington North Solar Farm back online (late Sunday afternoon 7th December and) Monday 8th December 2025" posted at 08-12-2025 08:40:00 AM is: Wellington North Solar Farm was taken offline after a grassfire on Saturday 6th December 2025. It resumed generating late Sunday afternoon (7th December) and by Monday morning 8th December it was ramping back toward maximum capacity, with ST PASA data indicating no long-term damage. The outage lasted roughly two days; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 6 December 2025 to 8 December 2025 (approximately 2 days). Its root_cause is: grassfire. Its accident scale is: single solar farm (capacity not specified). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 6-8 December 2025. Its affected region is: Wellington North, within the National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely local grid and electricity consumers). Its cause type is: external event (fire). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: grassfire on site.

title: Wellington North Solar Farm offline in ST PASA data from Saturday 6th December 2025, following grassfire
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Wellington North Solar Farm offline in ST PASA data from Saturday 6th December 2025, following grassfire" posted at 07-12-2025 03:30:00 PM is: On Saturday 6 December 2025, the Wellington North Solar Farm tripped offline at the 13:15 dispatch interval following a grassfire, with its ST PASA availability set to 0 MW for the week ahead; the unit’s bidding shows a reduction of LOCL and ELAV to zero and the auto-bidder stopped, while no official statement has been released by operator LightsourceBP, though media reports (e.g., Perry Williams in The Australian) link the event to a heatwave.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1  
Its category is: Extreme weather  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 6 December 2025 for at least one week  
Its root_cause is: Grassfire  
Its accident scale is: Single solar farm unit tripped offline  
Its dates is: 06-12-2025  
Its affected region is: Unknown (likely within NEM, possibly New South Wales)  
Its affected key users is: AEMO and market participants  
Its cause type is: External event (grassfire)  
Its causes is: Grassfire at Wellington North Solar Farm causing unit trip

title: Lengthy unplanned outage at Yallourn unit 2 extended another 19 days – expected RTS now 12th February 2026
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-08-26 YWPS2 unplanned outage due to 'LP turbine issues'
