classified_content: The summary of the news "Lengthy unplanned outage at Yallourn unit 2 extended another 19 days – expected RTS now 12th February 2026" posted at 07-12-2025 02:47:00 PM is: EnergyAustralia reported a 19-day extension to the unplanned outage at Yallourn unit 2 (YWPS2) due to low-pressure turbine issues first identified in August 2025, with the new expected return to service date pushed to 12 February 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from August 2025 to February 2026 (long-term). Its root_cause is: LP turbine issues. Its accident scale is: unplanned outage of one unit at a coal-fired power station. Its dates is: 2025-08-26 (original outage) to 2026-02-12 (new RTS). Its affected region is: Australia (National Electricity Market, Yallourn power station in Victoria). Its affected key users is: electricity market participants and consumers. Its cause type is: mechanical failure. Its causes is: low-pressure turbine issues.

title: Actual MSL2 in South Australia (not the first time) on Sunday 7th December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Actual MSL2 in South Australia (not the first time) on Sunday 7th December 2025" posted at 07-12-2025 02:25:00 PM is: On 7 December 2025, AEMO declared an Actual MSL2 condition in South Australia due to forecast operational demand dropping to -157 MW at 13:00, below the MSL2 threshold of -19 MW. This condition, marking the second such occurrence after a similar event on 11 November 2025, is expected to persist until 15:30, posing an elevated risk of system insecurity from insufficient demand. The low demand is largely attributed to high non-scheduled generation (e.g., rooftop solar) creating a surplus; Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 07-12-2025 from 11:02 to 15:30 NEM time. Its root_cause is: Insufficient operational demand due to high renewable generation causing oversupply, leading to a minimum system load condition. Its accident scale is: Regional event affecting the entire South Australia region. Its dates is: 07-12-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: All market participants, generators, and consumers in South Australia (system security concern). Its cause type is: Operational/technical condition. Its causes is: 1) Forecast operational demand of -157 MW; 2) Demand falling below the MSL2 threshold of -19 MW; 3) AEMO detecting an elevated risk of insecure operation due to insufficient demand.

title: Increase in minimum LHS Factors in Constraint Equations gone live Tuesday 2nd December 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Increase in minimum LHS Factors in Constraint Equations gone live Tuesday 2nd December 2025" posted at 03-12-2025 04:47:00 PM is: AEMO increased the minimum left-hand side (LHS) factors in constraint equations from 0.07 to 0.15, effective 2 December 2025, as part of the Improving Security Frameworks reform. This change aims to prevent low-coefficient interconnectors from being disproportionately constrained, which previously led to inefficient dispatch outcomes and price distortions, such as counterprice flows on the VIC1-NSW1 interconnector. The adjustment is expected to reduce extreme changes in interconnector flows and better target curtailment of high-coefficient generators. Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 2 December 2025 onward. Its root_cause is: Low minimum LHS factors (0.07) allowed interconnectors with small coefficients to be disproportionately constrained, causing inefficient dispatch and price distortions. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 02-12-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Generators, interconnectors, market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory change. Its causes is: Increase in minimum LHS factors from 0.07 to 0.15 to reduce disproportionate interconnector curtailment.

title: ‘Blackout bingo’ in the media again this week … this time with Victoria and Yallourn
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news “Blackout bingo” in the media again this week … this time with Victoria and Yallourn posted at 03-12-2025 01:45:00 PM is: The article highlights two media reports on blackout risks: one warns of NSW blackouts if Eraring closes in 2027, and another warns Victoria faces load-shedding due to the looming closure of Yallourn power station, reflecting recurring media focus on potential supply shortfalls. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short to medium term (2027 for NSW; near-term for Victoria). Its root_cause is: Planned closure of coal-fired power stations (Eraring and Yallourn). Its accident scale is: Unknown (potential blackout risk). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 03-12-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales and Victoria. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers (residential, commercial, industrial). Its cause type is: Generator retirement. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Closure of Eraring Power Station; Closure of Yallourn Power Station.

title: AEMO declares Scheduling Error – related to the ‘Self-forecast vendor issue’ experienced on 19th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO declares Scheduling Error – related to the ‘Self-forecast vendor issue’ experienced on 19th August 2025" posted at 02-12-2025 03:14:00 PM is: On 19 August 2025, a software update by a self-forecast vendor erroneously set the Uninterrupted Intermittent Generation Forecast (UIGF) to zero for 34 semi-scheduled plants across the National Electricity Market (NEM). This caused a generation surplus of approximately 1,550 MW, as the units continued to output power without a Semi-Dispatch Cap, leading to a large frequency spike that exceeded the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) upper limit of 50.15 Hz. The AEMO later declared a Scheduling Error on 27 November 2025, noting potential compensation for affected parties that provided lower Primary Frequency Response. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 19 August 2025 (the day of the frequency spike). Its root_cause is: A self-forecast provider software update incorrectly set UIGF to zero for 34 semi-scheduled units. Its accident scale is: 34 semi-scheduled plants affected, generation surplus of ~1,550 MW, frequency exceeded 50.15 Hz. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 19 August 2025. Its affected region is: Mainland Australia (NEM). Its affected key users are: Semi-scheduled generators (34 units) and units providing lower Primary Frequency Response. Its cause type is: Software update error / vendor issue. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Self-forecast provider software update set UIGF to zero for 34 semi-scheduled plants; absence of Semi-Dispatch Cap allowed non-zero output; resulting generation surplus drove frequency above NOFB.

title: How collective under-performance of Semi-Scheduled Solar Farms weakened frequency on Sunday afternoon 30th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How collective under-performance of Semi-Scheduled Solar Farms weakened frequency on Sunday afternoon 30th November 2025" posted at 02-12-2025 12:56:00 PM is: On Sunday 30 November 2025, four discrete periods of frequency weakness occurred around sunset (17:20, 17:35, 17:50, 18:10 NEM time), caused by collective under-performance of semi-scheduled solar farms. Battery and coal units provided corrective response, but the pattern highlights scalability concerns for grid stability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 30 November 2025, 17:20–18:10 NEM time. Its root_cause is: Collective under-performance of Semi-Scheduled Solar Farms during sunset. Its accident scale is: Four discrete periods of frequency weakness (minor to moderate frequency deviations). Its dates is: 30-11-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators, electricity consumers, and market participants relying on frequency stability. Its cause type is: Operational / Technical (generation shortfall). Its causes is: 1) Underperformance of semi-scheduled solar farms during sunset; 2) Insufficient inertial response leading to frequency drops; 3) Reliance on battery and coal units for corrective actions.

title: What the hail is happening with Brisbane’s weather?
author: Anthony Cornelius
classified_content: The summary of the news "What the hail is happening with Brisbane’s weather?" posted at 02-12-2025 10:34:00 AM is: The article explains that Brisbane and Southeast Queensland have experienced an exceptional hail and thunderstorm season in 2025, with ten days of giant hail in six weeks, driven by a combination of climate drivers including a sudden stratospheric warming event, a negative Indian Ocean Dipole, a weak La Niña, marine heatwaves, and frequent upper troughs, with the pattern expected to persist until mid-December, posing risks to infrastructure such as solar farms and businesses. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Late October 2025 to mid-December 2025. Its root_cause is: Multiple climate drivers: sudden stratospheric warming, negative Indian Ocean Dipole, weak La Niña/La Niña Modoki, marine heatwave, and upper troughs. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-12-2025 10:34:00 AM. Its affected region is: Brisbane and Southeast Queensland, Australia. Its affected key users is: Insurers, businesses, solar farms. Its cause type is: Natural/climate. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Sudden stratospheric warming, negative Indian Ocean Dipole, weak La Niña, marine heatwave, upper troughs.

title: Why network outages matter for generator and storage revenue, risk and investment decisions
author: Nadali Mahmoudi
classified_content: The summary of the news "Why network outages matter for generator and storage revenue, risk and investment decisions" posted at 01-12-2025 is: The article explains how increasing network outages in Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM) create both opportunities and risks for battery energy storage systems (BESS) by driving price volatility while posing curtailment threats. Using a July 2029 case study of a Woolooga to Palmwoods 275kV line outage in Queensland, it demonstrates that ignoring network constraints in capacity models overestimates wind capacity and underestimates the need for gas generation. The outage forces a low-voltage constraint that restricts central Queensland coal output, requiring gas-fired generation to meet demand, thereby increasing volatility and revenue risk for BESS operators. The article stresses the importance of incorporating network constraints into market modelling for accurate investment decisions.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: July 8–12, 2029 (modelled period); ongoing trend of increasing outage frequency. Its root_cause is: Outage of the Woolooga to Palmwoods 275kV transmission line in Queensland, triggering a low-voltage constraint that limits central Queensland coal generation and forces reliance on gas. Its accident scale is: Single transmission line outage (Woolooga to Palmwoods 275kV). Its dates are: Article date: 01-12-2025; Event dates: 08-07-2029 to 12-07-2029. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (particularly the Woolooga to Palmwoods corridor and southern Queensland). Its affected key users is: BESS operators, developers, investors, and off-takers; gas generators in southern Queensland. Its cause type is: Transmission line outage (operational/technical). Its causes are: Increased frequency of network outages in the NEM; specific outage of the Woolooga to Palmwoods 275kV line; resultant binding of the low-voltage constraint “Avoid overload of Woolooga to Gympie 132kV on trip of Woolooga to South Pine 275kV line.”

title: Mainland Frequency dragged downwards around sunset, on Sunday 30th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland Frequency dragged downwards around sunset, on Sunday 30th November 2025" posted at 01-12-2025 03:17:00 PM is: A downward drag on system frequency occurred around sunset on Sunday 30th November 2025 in the Mainland grid, attributed to potential under-performance of variable renewable energy (VRE) sources, especially rooftop PV and possibly collective under-performance of Semi-Scheduled units, highlighting forecasting challenges for frequency control. Other factors may also have contributed. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Event on 30 Nov 2025, impact immediate within hours. Its root_cause is: Under-performance of VRE (rooftop PV and Semi-Scheduled units) and forecasting inadequacy. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 30-11-2025. Its affected region is: Mainland (likely Australian National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational/forecasting failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Under-performance of rooftop PV, collective under-performance of Semi-Scheduled units, forecasting challenges.

title: AEMO releases ‘2025 Transition Plan for System Security’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO releases ‘2025 Transition Plan for System Security’" posted at 01-12-2025 09:11:00 AM is: AEMO's 2025 Transition Plan for System Security (TPSS) outlines upcoming system security challenges in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM), including system strength requirements in New South Wales (first flagged in 2021) and minimum system load risks in Queensland and other states from 2026 onward, calling for new investments and reforms to maintain reliability and co-optimize costs, with media linking the report to potential further delays in the closure of Eraring Power Station. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing – with specific transition points identified for system strength in NSW (from 2021) and minimum system load risks from 2026 in Queensland and other states. Its root_cause is: Transition to renewable energy and retirement of coal-fired generation leading to system security risks such as declining inertia, system strength, and minimum load issues. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident; plan addresses systemic risks). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 01-12-2025 (same as news date, though report covers future dates). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) regions, specifically New South Wales, Queensland, and other states. Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, transmission network service providers, generators, and market operators. Its cause type is: Systemic/transitional (policy and market evolution). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Retirement of coal-fired power stations (e.g., Eraring); (2) Increasing penetration of renewable energy and distributed energy resources; (3) Need to manage system strength, inertia, and minimum system load in the evolving grid.

title: AFR headline ‘AEMO warns of NSW blackouts if Eraring closes in 2027’ attracts attention
author: Paul McArdle
topic: AEMO's annual 'Transition Plan for System Security' (TPSS)
classified_content: The summary of the news "AFR headline ‘AEMO warns of NSW blackouts if Eraring closes in 2027’ attracts attention" posted at 01-12-2025 09:00:00 AM is: AEMO’s 2025 Transition Plan for System Security warns that New South Wales faces blackouts if the Eraring coal-fired power plant closes as scheduled in 2027, highlighting a significant gap between the planned closure and the availability of replacement generation capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 2027 (potential blackouts if Eraring closes). Its root_cause is: Planned closure of Eraring coal plant without adequate replacement generation to meet demand. Its accident scale is: Unknown (potential large-scale power outages affecting NSW). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 2027. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in NSW, including residential, commercial, and industrial users. Its cause type is: Planned closure of a coal-fired power plant. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO analysis shows that retirement of Eraring by 2027 would create a supply shortfall; insufficient replacement generation (renewables, storage, or gas) is being built or planned to compensate; grid reliability standards may be breached, leading to blackouts.

title: One example of why some batteries were charging during price spikes over the past couple of days in NSW
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "One example of why some batteries were charging during price spikes over the past couple of days in NSW" posted at 27-11-2025 03:58:00 PM is: On 25 November 2025, a sudden cloud bank over Sydney caused a rapid drop in rooftop PV generation, increasing net demand in NSW and altering transmission line flows, which tightened the feedback constraint N::N_CNLT_2, forcing the 100 MW Capital BESS to charge at a price of $14,974.54/MWh to relieve the constraint; the battery then rebid its load as unavailable in the next interval to avoid further losses. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (during the 12:15 dispatch interval on 25 November 2025). Its root_cause is: sudden drop in rooftop PV generation due to cloud cover causing rapid demand increase and tightening of a feedback constraint. Its accident scale is: small-scale (affecting one battery and a few hundred MW of curtailed generation). Its dates are: 25-11-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia. Its affected key users is: Capital BESS (100 MW battery). Its cause type is: operational/weather-induced grid constraint. Its causes are: 1) a cloud bank over Sydney reduced rooftop PV output; 2) net market demand surged; 3) transmission line flows changed; 4) the N::N_CNLT_2 feedback constraint tightened its RHS; 5) the dispatch engine instructed the battery to charge at high price to relieve the constraint.

title: A look at mainland grid frequency yesterday, November 26th, 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "A look at mainland grid frequency yesterday, November 26th, 2025" posted at 27-11-2025 02:29:00 PM is: The article examines mainland grid frequency on November 26, 2025, following a price spike, noting that frequency remained within the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) with a low point of 49.853 Hz at 10:51 and a high point around 12:40 indicating supply exceeding demand, though root causes of the deviations are not analyzed. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: November 26, 2025, during 09:00-14:00 (short-term intraday). Its root_cause is: Unknown (supply-demand imbalance inferred but not specified). Its accident scale is: Minor frequency excursion within NOFB. Its dates is: 2025-11-26. Its affected region is: Mainland grid (likely Australian National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: All electricity market participants on the mainland grid. Its cause type is: Grid frequency event. Its causes is: Low frequency at 10:51 due to supply shortage; high frequency around 12:40 due to excess supply (observed events, causes not studied).

title: Unpacking some of the drivers of volatility in NSW today, on Wednesday 26th Nov 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unpacking some of the drivers of volatility in NSW today, on Wednesday 26th Nov 2025" posted at 26-11-2025 01:10:00 PM is: On 26 November 2025, the New South Wales (NSW) electricity market experienced extreme intraday volatility, with the spot price spiking to the Market Price Cap ($21,300/MWh) at 11:00 and then plunging to near the Market Price Floor (-$1,000/MWh) within one hour, setting a record intra-day spread of $21,299.99/MWh. The volatility was driven by a convergence of factors: high temperatures pushing up demand, fast-moving storm clouds that suddenly reduced rooftop solar PV output (adding forecasting uncertainty for AEMO), a transmission outage on the Avon–Marulan 330 kV line, binding thermal constraints (e.g., N>>16_8_39, N>NIL_969, N>>NIL_85_86_S, N>NIL_94T, N>NIL_94K_1) limiting generator dispatch, interconnector counter-price flows and Negative Residue Management constraints on VNI and QNI, and pre-existing generator outages—including the 850 MW Waratah BESS (transformer incident, unavailable until May 2026, and currently not providing SIPS services), the 685 MW Bayswater Unit 1, and the 680 MW Vales Point Unit 5 (both weeks away from return). The price cap was exceeded in the physical dispatch price due to binding constraints, and curtailment of variable renewable energy (VRE) was extensive, with over half of semi-scheduled VRE availability curtailed during the spike. The event highlights the compounding effects of weather, generator failures, transmission constraints, and system instability on wholesale electricity prices. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Same day, intraday volatility (26 Nov 2025). Its root_cause is: Convergence of sudden cloud cover reducing rooftop solar, high temperature demand, generator outages (Waratah BESS transformer failure, Bayswater Unit 1, Vales Point Unit 5), transmission line outage (Avon–Marulan 330 kV), and binding network constraints causing extreme price volatility. Its accident scale is: Record intra-day price spread of $21,299.99/MWh between market price cap and floor within one hour; multiple dispatch intervals at extreme prices (four at cap, four at floor); physical dispatch price exceeding cap due to constraints. Its dates (if different from main date) is: 26-11-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants (generators, storage operators), electricity consumers in NSW, rooftop PV owners indirectly. Its cause type is: Combination of weather, technical failures, and operational constraints. Its causes is: High temperatures; sudden cloud cover from storm reducing rooftop PV output; pre-existing generator outages (Waratah BESS transformer incident, Bayswater Unit 1, Vales Point Unit 5); transmission line outage on Avon–Marulan 330 kV; binding thermal constraints (N>>16_8_39, N>NIL_969, N>>NIL_85_86_S, N>NIL_94T, N>NIL_94K_1); interconnector counter-price flows and Negative Residue Management constraints on VNI and QNI; and AEMO forecasting challenges due to rapid cloud movement.

title: Daytime price volatility in NSW on November 26th, 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Daytime price volatility in NSW on November 26th, 2025" posted at 26-11-2025 11:50:00 AM is: On November 26, 2025, electricity prices in New South Wales (NSW) reached the Market Price Cap (MPC) of $20,300/MWh for four successive trading intervals from 11:00 to 11:15. The price spike was accompanied by counter-price flows from NSW into Victoria, interconnector imports from Queensland reaching limits, and notable generation changes—battery output increased while wind generation decreased and solar output rose. This event highlights a temporary supply-demand imbalance causing extreme price volatility in the NSW region. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Intraday on 26 November 2025, specifically the four 5-minute intervals from 11:00 to 11:15. Its root_cause is: Supply-demand imbalance due to fluctuating renewable generation (wind drop, solar rise) and interconnector constraints. Its accident scale is: Localized price spike in the NSW market region. Its dates is: 26-11-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia, within the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Wholesale market participants, generators, retailers, and end-consumers exposed to spot prices. Its cause type is: Market and operational factors. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Reduction in wind generation, 2. Increase in solar generation, 3. Increase in battery output, 4. Interconnector from Queensland to NSW operating at its limit, 5. Counter-price flows from NSW to Victoria indicating congestion.

title: Record intra-day price spread as the NSW spot price goes near the market floor, after hitting the market cap on Tuesday 25th Nov 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Record intra-day price spread as the NSW spot price goes near the market floor, after hitting the market cap on Tuesday 25th Nov 2025" posted at 25-11-2025 03:55:00 PM is: On November 25, 2025, the New South Wales (NSW) region in the National Electricity Market experienced the largest intra-day price spread in NEM history, with the spot price spiking to $20,300/MWh (market cap) at the 12:20 dispatch interval and then plunging to –$719.65/MWh (near the floor of –$1,000/MWh) at 15:45, a swing of $21,019.65. The extreme volatility was linked to unforecast constraint equations, limiting the battery fleet's ability to arbitrage; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Intra-day (single day), with immediate price fluctuations but no longer-term impact specified. Its root_cause is: Unforecast constraint equations in the AEMO pre-dispatch model causing sharp supply-demand imbalance and price spikes/dips. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 25-11-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: Battery fleet (noted as having limited response window). Its cause type is: Market/system operation. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Three interesting constraint equations affecting NSW dispatch; (2) P5 pre-dispatch model did not forecast the initial price spike; (3) Subsequent collapse to negative prices due to oversupply or network constraints.

classified_content: The summary of the news "Price spikes up to the MPC in NSW, seemingly out of the blue, during the middle of Tuesday, 25th Nov 2025" posted at 25-11-2025 02:00:00 PM is: On 25 November 2025, the NSW spot price unexpectedly spiked to the Market Price Cap (MPC) during two dispatch intervals (12:15 and 12:20) due to a sharp rise in market demand caused by a large cloud bank reducing rooftop solar output over Sydney, combined with binding network constraints from outages (Lower Tumut–Canberra 330 kV line, Parkes transformer) that limited supply response. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-lived spike from 12:15 to 12:20 on 25 Nov 2025, with projected further spikes later that afternoon. Its root_cause is: Cloud bank reducing rooftop PV generation, causing sudden increase in net market demand, and simultaneous binding network constraints limiting generation response. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 25-11-2025. Its affected region is: NSW, specifically the Sydney metropolitan area. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Combination of weather (cloud cover) and network outages/constraints. Its causes is: 1. Large cloud bank reducing rooftop PV output across Sydney. 2. Sudden increase in net market demand. 3. Binding transient stability constraint due to outage of Lower Tumut–Canberra 330 kV line. 4. Binding thermal constraint in northern NSW (N>>NIL_85_86_S). 5. Binding thermal constraint due to outage of Parkes transformer (N>PKTX_TX_NIL).

title: Energex report nearly 100,000 customers still without power this morning in South-East Queensland after last night’s storms
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Energex report nearly 100,000 customers still without power this morning in South-East Queensland after last night’s storms" posted at 25-11-2025 10:20:00 AM is: On 24 November 2025, severe thunderstorms with destructive winds exceeding 110 km/h, giant hailstones up to 12 cm, and over 880,000 lightning strikes swept through South-East Queensland, causing extensive damage to electricity infrastructure. Initially 161,000 customers lost power; by the morning of 25 November, approximately 96,000 remained without power with over 600 powerlines down. Crews restored 64,000 customers overnight, but some areas may not see power restored until the evening of 26 November.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Extreme weather; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (24-26 November 2025); Its root_cause is: Severe thunderstorms with destructive winds, giant hail, and dense lightning causing physical damage to power lines and infrastructure; Its accident scale is: Up to 161,000 customers affected, with 96,000 still without power and over 600 powerlines down; Its dates is: 24-11-2025 (event date); Its affected region is: South-East Queensland, Australia; Its affected key users is: Residential customers and schools (11 schools closed); Its cause type is: Natural disaster (severe weather); Its causes is: Destructive winds >110 km/h, giant hailstones up to 12 cm in diameter, dense lightning strikes (880,000 recorded).

title: Further thoughts (and questions) about Waratah BESS, two weeks after the snowball started to roll…
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Further thoughts (and questions) about Waratah BESS, two weeks after the snowball started to roll…" posted at 21-11-2025 01:34:00 PM is: This article delves into the lengthy unplanned partial outage of the Waratah BESS, beginning with a catastrophic transformer failure over the weekend of 18-20 October 2025, which reduced availability and led to an extended outage until at least May 2026. The author traces the chronology from the initial failure, through rebids and PASA data updates, to the public awareness sparked by an AFR article on 10-11 November 2025. Market impacts on NSW hedge contracts were difficult to discern, while Akaysha Energy's subsequent update confirmed ongoing root cause analysis and a separate planned shutdown. The event highlights grid service vulnerabilities and raises questions about project timelines and contract awards.

Its **relevance level** is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its **category** is: grid/system instability.  
Its **Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market** is: October 2025 to at least May 2026 (ongoing).  
Its **root_cause** is: Catastrophic failure of a transformer (HVT3) at the Waratah BESS.  
Its **accident scale** is: Catastrophic failure, leading to a near full-year outage.  
Its **dates** (if different from the main date) is: Incident on 18-10-2025, extended outage announced on 07-11-2025.  
Its **affected region** is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia.  
Its **affected key users** are: AEMO, Transgrid, EnergyCo, and NSW electricity consumers reliant on System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS).  
Its **cause type** is: Equipment failure (transformer).  
Its **causes** (a summarized list of cause events) are:  
- Transformer failure (HVT3) during commissioning or operation.  
- Subsequent de-energisation of HVT2 for testing.  
- Root cause analysis ongoing, expected to take weeks or months.

title: Extending the tabulated history of significant (Frequency and/or AggROT) events into 2025 Q3 and 2025 Q4
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Is the Semi-Scheduled Category Sustainable or Scalable?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Extending the tabulated history of significant (Frequency and/or AggROT) events into 2025 Q3 and 2025 Q4" posted at 20-11-2025 08:00:00 PM is: This article extends the tabulated history of significant frequency and Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) events for semi-scheduled units in the National Electricity Market (NEM) into 2025 Q3 and Q4, documenting numerous frequency excursions (both within and outside the NOFB) and large AggROT values (exceeding 300 MW, 500 MW, or 600 MW). These events are attributed to a variety of causes including collective underperformance of solar farms (e.g., 31 Oct, 30 Oct, 16 Oct), generator trips (e.g., Callide B2 on 28 Oct, Loy Yang A1 on 30 Aug, Yallourn unit 2 on 26 Aug, CPP_4 on 26 Oct), AEMO data glitches causing phantom demand ramps (e.g., 6 Nov, 11 Nov), and other operational issues. The analysis raises concerns about the sustainability and scalability of the semi-scheduled category, as highlighted at the NEMdev 2025 conference and in relation to the Nelson Review. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing from early August 2025 to mid-November 2025. Its root_cause is: Multiple factors including variable renewable energy (VRE) underperformance, generator outages, and AEMO data system glitches. Its accident scale is: Multiple discrete events; some frequency drops exceeded the NOFB (e.g., 49.826 Hz on 16 Oct, 49.838 Hz on 26 Aug) and AggROT reached up to -1,678 MW (over-performance) on 19 Aug 2025. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Various dates from 2 August 2025 to 15 November 2025. Its affected region is: NEM mainland (primarily Victoria and Queensland, but affecting the entire NEM). Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants, semi-scheduled generators, and system operators. Its cause type is: Technical and operational. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Collective underperformance of solar farms (e.g., 31 Oct, 30 Oct, 16 Oct, 20 Sep, 27 Aug); 2) Generator trips (Callide B2, Loy Yang A1, Yallourn unit 2, CPP_4, Tallawarra B, WTAHB1, etc.); 3) AEMO data glitches causing phantom Victorian demand ramps (6 Nov, 11 Nov); 4) Other uninvestigated events (e.g., 3 Nov, 2 Nov, 1 Nov, 29 Oct, etc.) and occasional over-performance of semi-scheduled units (e.g., 14 Sep, 19 Aug).

title: A case study into reserve uncertainty during peak demand events in the NEM
author: Dan Lee
topic: 2024-01-22 QLD (massively higher!) all time max demand
classified_content: The summary of the news "A case study into reserve uncertainty during peak demand events in the NEM" posted at 20-11-2025 is: On January 22, 2024, Queensland experienced an unprecedented peak demand exceeding 11 GW due to extreme heat (42.5°C) and a sudden cloud band that caused a rapid collapse of rooftop solar output, leading to a steep net demand ramp. Reserve margins fell to just 4.09% (425 MW), requiring activation of demand response and distribution network outages (affecting 40,000+ properties) to avoid load shedding. Forecast errors of up to 750 MW at one hour ahead hindered reserve activation mechanisms, highlighting challenges of high DER penetration and forecasting limitations.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: Grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (hours) on 22 January 2024, with long-term implications for market design and forecasting; Its root_cause is: Extreme heatwave (42.5°C) combined with unexpected dense cloud cover reducing rooftop PV output by over 1,500 MW/hr, causing a rapid net demand surge that depleted reserve margins; Its accident scale is: Near miss of load shedding; excess generation fell to 425 MW (4.09% IR

title: How high did frequency get, on Tuesday 11th November 2025, when that gremlin re-appeared?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How high did frequency get, on Tuesday 11th November 2025, when that gremlin re-appeared?" posted at 19-11-2025 01:51:00 PM is: On Tuesday 11th November 2025, a frequency excursion occurred in the National Electricity Market (NEM), with high-speed readings from a Device 2 in Brisbane showing the frequency peaked at 50.152 Hz at 09:10:34.7 NEM time, just outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB). This deviation was part of a period above 50.14 Hz that lasted from 09:07:52.2 to 09:11:07.8, a duration of over 3 minutes. The event is attributed to a recurring "gremlin" interfering with the NEM Dispatch Engine (NEMDE). Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and during the morning of 11 November 2025. Its root_cause is: Systematic anomaly in NEMDE (likely software or control system error) causing a temporary frequency deviation. Its accident scale is: Unknown (single frequency excursion, no reported widespread outages). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 11-11-2025. Its affected region is: Brisbane (monitoring location) and likely the wider NEM. Its affected key users is: Unknown (general grid participants). Its cause type is: Technical/operational anomaly. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Recurrence of an unexplained "gremlin" in NEMDE, triggering a frequency excursion above the NOFB threshold.

title: The bottom falls out of SA ‘Market Demand’ unexpectedly, on Tuesday 18th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "The bottom falls out of SA ‘Market Demand’ unexpectedly, on Tuesday 18th November 2025" posted at 19-11-2025 08:07:00 AM is: On Tuesday 18 November 2025, South Australia experienced an unexpected and steep drop in Market Demand, plunging from forecast levels by about 600 MW (80%) to a low of 150 MW around midday. The main cause was likely a sudden clearing of clouds that unleashed a large, unforecast surge in rooftop solar photovoltaic (PV) generation, overwhelming AEMO’s earlier demand forecasts and triggering an Actual Minimum System Load Level 1 (MSL1) condition—a grid stability alert—that lasted from approximately 13:00 to 14:00 NEM time. This event highlights the ongoing challenge of forecasting variable renewable energy (VRE) output.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 18 November 2025, approximately 11:30 to 14:00 NEM time (midday impact).; Its root_cause is: Unexpected high output from rooftop solar PV due to sudden cloud clearing, causing a sharp reduction in net demand.; Its accident scale is: Not an accident; a demand forecast error of about 600 MW (80% below forecast), leading to an MSL1 advisory condition.; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: Main event date: 18 November 2025.; Its affected region is: South Australia (SA region of the National Electricity Market).; Its affected key users is: AEMO, grid operators, and potentially residential/commercial customers with rooftop PV (as generators), but specific users not named.; Its cause type is: Weather-related (cloud cover variability) and renewable generation forecast uncertainty.; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Clearing of clouds over South Australia around 11:30 NEM time. 2) Resulting surge in rooftop PV output far exceeding AEMO’s forecast. 3) Consequent rapid decline in Market Demand to 150 MW, triggering an MSL1 condition.

title: Expected RTS for Yallourn 2 slips a month – now offline until 24th January 2026
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-08-26 YWPS2 unplanned outage due to 'LP turbine issues'
classified_content: The summary of the news "Expected RTS for Yallourn 2 slips a month – now offline until 24th January 2026" posted at 15-11-2025 12:20:00 PM is: Yallourn Unit 2 (YWPS2) experienced an unplanned outage on 26 August 2025 due to low-pressure turbine issues. Initially slated to return by 15 December 2025, the expected return-to-service date was pushed back by approximately six weeks to 24 January 2026, as revealed in market data updates on 14 November 2025. EnergyAustralia has not yet issued a public statement. The extended outage adds to supply pressures in the National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly alongside the recent Waratah BESS failure. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 26 August 2025 to 24 January 2026. Its root_cause is: LP turbine issues (low-pressure turbine failure). Its accident scale is: Unplanned outage of a single coal-fired unit (approximately 375 MW capacity). Its dates are: Trip on 26 August 2025; Expected return on 24 January 2026; News published on 15 November 2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (National Electricity Market region). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers in Victoria and the broader NEM, grid operators, and market participants. Its cause type is: Technical/mechanical failure. Its causes is: Low-pressure turbine issues leading to extended repair and replacement timeline.

title: AEMO frequency monitoring report provides some details of frequency event (due to self-forecast glitch) on 19th August 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO frequency monitoring report provides some details of frequency event (due to self-forecast glitch) on 19th August 2025" posted at 13-11-2025 02:25:00 PM is: AEMO's Q3 2025 frequency monitoring report details a frequency spike on 19 August 2025 caused by a self-forecasting error from a single provider that affected several semi-scheduled generators in the National Electricity Market (NEM). The glitch led to 0 MW dispatch targets for these units, but they continued generating near previous levels, creating an oversupply of about 1,700 MW that pushed mainland frequency to 50.21 Hz, exceeding the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) for over five minutes. The provider suppressed its self-forecasts, allowing AEMO’s internal forecasting systems to restore accurate dispatch, while AEMO increased regulation FCAS requirements. Frequency stabilized after 1,139.2 seconds, and corrective actions including transitioning sites to AEMO forecasts and provider process improvements were implemented. This non-contingent system-level failure prompted AEMO to propose that the Reliability Panel consider establishing explicit frequency performance specifications for such events. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: The event occurred on 19 August 2025, with frequency deviation lasting approximately 19 minutes; immediate operational impacts were mitigated, but corrective actions and policy review are ongoing. Its root_cause is: A self-forecasting software glitch at a single forecasting provider that caused multiple semi-scheduled generators to receive zero dispatch targets while they continued generating, leading to oversupply and frequency rise. Its accident scale is: Approximately 1,700 MW generation surplus relative to dispatch target at the peak. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 19 August 2025 (event date); news published 13 November 2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland. Its affected key users is: Semi-scheduled generators (renewable sites) whose self-forecasts were affected. Its cause type is: Technical/software error (self-forecast glitch). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Self-forecast provider incorrectly issued 0 MW availability forecasts for multiple renewable sites; 2) Affected units continued generating near previous dispatch targets, resulting in 1,700 MW oversupply; 3) This led to frequency exceeding the NOFB to 50.21 Hz; 4) Provider suppressed all self-forecasts, AEMO reverted to internal forecasts and increased regulation FCAS; 5) Corrective actions included transitioning sites to AEMO forecasts and provider implementing process improvements.

title: Trying to understand the errors in ‘Initial Supply’ (on Tue 11th and Thu 6th November) as the source of the gremlins
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Trying to understand the errors in ‘Initial Supply’ (on Tue 11th and Thu 6th November) as the source of the gremlins" posted at 13-11-2025 10:32:00 AM is: This article investigates two recent frequency spike events in the Australian National Electricity Market on November 6 and November 11, 2025, caused by errors in the 'Initial Supply' data for Victoria. By cross-checking bottom-up calculations with AEMO's aggregated data, the author confirms that non-zero errors in INITIALSUPPLY align perfectly with the periods of frequency deviations, indicating a transient gremlin in the data gathering (step 1a) or pre-processing (step 1b) steps that inflated market demand; the exact root cause remains unidentified. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the dispatch intervals on November 6 and November 11, 2025. Its root_cause is: Error in the INITIALSUPPLY calculation for Victoria due to a transient gremlin in data gathering or pre-processing steps. Its accident scale is: Unknown (involved 4 dispatch intervals on 6 Nov and 14 on 11 Nov). Its dates is: November 6 and November 11, 2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australian National Electricity Market. Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely AEMO, generators, and consumers). Its cause type is: Data/calculation error. Its causes is: Error in INITIALSUPPLY data leading to inflated market demand; potential issues in step 1a (data gathering) or step 1b (pre-processing) of the dispatch interval calculation.

title: How often have we seen these gremlins, injecting ‘phantom’ demand and driving frequency high? … a 1st pass
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "How often have we seen these gremlins, injecting ‘phantom’ demand and driving frequency high? … a 1st pass" posted at 12-11-2025 07:37:00 PM is: The article documents repeated occurrences of phantom demand being injected into AEMO's dispatch process, causing NEMDE to over-dispatch supply and drive frequency spikes; two recent events on 6 and 11 November 2025 saw glitch sizes of approximately 1,812 MW and 1,130 MW respectively, with earlier incidents dating back to 2024, all attributed to scheduling errors. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate – during dispatch intervals when glitches occur, with frequency deviations lasting minutes. Its root_cause is: Scheduling error or data glitch causing phantom demand injection into NEMDE. Its accident scale is: Largest recent glitch sizes: ~1,812 MW (6 Nov 2025) and ~1,130 MW (11 Nov 2025); historical maximum ~3,100 MW (9 Dec 2024). Its dates are: 6 November 2025, 11 November 2025, 19 February 2025, 31 January 2025, 9 December 2024, and period 24 January to 7 March 2024. Its affected region is: Victoria (primary) and New South Wales. Its affected key users is: AEMO, generators, market participants, and end consumers (due to frequency excursions outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band). Its cause type is: Operational glitch / scheduling error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Phantom demand injection into AEMO's NEMDE dispatch process, leading to over-dispatch of supply and frequency spikes.

title: AEMO (and the BOM) warning about possible ‘Severe Space Weather’ (at G4, possible G5) in next ~24 hours
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO (and the BOM) warning about possible ‘Severe Space Weather’ (at G4, possible G5) in next ~24 hours" posted at 12-11-2025 12:33:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice on 10th November 2025 warning of potential severe space weather (G4 geomagnetic storm with possible G5) within the next 24 hours, based on BOM forecasts. The storm could cause widespread voltage control problems, false tripping of protective systems, and in extreme cases, grid collapse or transformer damage, as outlined by NOAA scales. The event is driven by a strong shock in the solar wind from recent solar activity.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, within 24 hours from 12-Nov-2025, lasting hours to days.; Its root_cause is: Severe geomagnetic storm caused by solar activity (coronal mass ejection/shock in solar wind).; Its accident scale is: G4–G5 on the NOAA space weather scale (Severe to Extreme).; Its dates are: 12-Nov-2025 (event forecast date).; Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly mid to southern parts of Australia.; Its affected key users are: Electricity grid operators, power system assets, transformers, and all connected consumers.; Its cause type is: Natural phenomenon (space weather).; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Strong shock in solar wind detected at 11/2338UT; solar activity expected at R2-R3; resulting in G4 geomagnetic storm conditions with chance of G5; solar radiation storm at S2 expected to reach S3.

title: AEMO directs again in South Australia for Minimum System Load – Wednesday 12th November 2025 (second day in a row)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO directs again in South Australia for Minimum System Load – Wednesday 12th November 2025 (second day in a row)" posted at 12-11-2025 is: On 12 November 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued a second consecutive direction to AGL SA Generation’s Torrens Island Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in South Australia to remain synchronised and follow dispatch targets from 07:00 to 14:30 hrs, in response to a forecast Minimum System Load (MSL) condition that created an elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state. This market intervention, mandated under clause 4.8.9(a1)(1) of the National Electricity Rules, declared the trading intervals as intervention events without intervention pricing. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 12 November 2025 (07:00 to 14:30). Its root_cause is: Forecast minimum system load leading to elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 12-11-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO and AGL SA Generation (Torrens Island BESS). Its cause type is: Grid stability management (minimum system load intervention). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Forecast minimum system load, Insufficient demand to maintain secure operating state].

title: More, about noisy MSL warnings for South Australia … and an Actual MSL2 and AEMO Direction on Tuesday 11th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "More, about noisy MSL warnings for South Australia … and an Actual MSL2 and AEMO Direction on Tuesday 11th November 2025" posted at 11-11-2025 12:50:00 PM is: On Tuesday 11th November 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator issued directions to the Torrens Island BESS and declared actual Minimum System Load (MSL) conditions (MSL1 and MSL2) in South Australia due to extremely low and even negative demand forecasts, creating an elevated risk of insufficient demand for maintaining a secure operating state. The directions and MSL alerts also warned of forecast MSL2 conditions continuing on 12th November 2025. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate and ongoing (11-12 November 2025). Its root_cause is: Insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state due to high renewable generation penetration (solar/wind) driving operational demand below MSL thresholds. Its accident scale is: Not applicable (system condition, not an accident). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 11/11/2025 and 12/11/2025. Its affected region is: South Australia (SA). Its affected key users are: AEMO, AGL SA Generation (Torrens Island BESS), and all National Electricity Market participants. Its cause type is: Minimum System Load event (operational). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: Forecast operational demand dropping to -5 MW and below MSL1/MSL2 thresholds; high renewable generation causing negative demand; AEMO direction to BESS to remain synchronized; actual MSL2 condition declared at 12:36 hrs.

title: Kogan Creek trips (on Tuesday morning 11th November 2025) … and ironically helps frequency
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Kogan Creek trips (on Tuesday morning 11th November 2025) … and ironically helps frequency" posted at 11-11-2025 10:25:00 AM is: On Tuesday 11 November 2025, a unit at the Kogan Creek power station tripped unexpectedly, ironically providing frequency relief to a grid that was experiencing an over-frequency condition, thereby helping to avoid a possible excursion outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band. The unit had just returned from a planned outage the previous day and was expected back online later that evening.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: turbine/generator failures.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (same day, expected return to service that evening).; Its root_cause is: Unknown.; Its accident scale is: Minor (single unit trip).; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 11-11-2025.; Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (Kogan Creek) and potentially broader National Electricity Market (NEM).; Its affected key users is: CS Energy (owner), AEMO (grid operator).; Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (trip).; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unit tripped unexpectedly during operation; no specific cause identified in the article.

title: Has it happened again … have gremlins invaded NEMDE and triggered a frequency excursion (Tuesday 11th November 2025)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Has it happened again … have gremlins invaded NEMDE and triggered a frequency excursion (Tuesday 11th November 2025)?" posted at 11-11-2025 09:41:00 AM is: On Tuesday 11th November 2025, a repeat incident occurred where erroneous input data for Victoria’s Market Demand caused a large 1,130 MW drop at the 09:15 dispatch interval, leading to a frequency excursion in the NEM, similar to an event on 6th November 2025; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate, within the dispatch interval (minutes); Its root_cause is: erroneous input data for Victoria's Market Demand (gremlins in NEMDE); Its accident scale is: 1,130 MW drop in Market Demand; Its dates is: 11-11-2025; Its affected region is: Victoria and the broader National Electricity Market (NEM); Its affected key users is: Unknown; Its cause type is: data/system input error; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: (1) Erroneous input data for Victoria's Market Demand, (2) A 1,130 MW drop in Market Demand at the 09:15 dispatch interval, (3) Consequent frequency excursion.

title: Angela’s article in AFR answers some of our "What’s up with Waratah BESS?" question
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-11-07 long unplanned outage at Waratah BESS?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Angela’s article in AFR answers some of our "What’s up with Waratah BESS?" question" posted at 11-11-2025 08:53:00 AM is: The article details a catastrophic transformer failure at the Waratah BESS during Hold Point 5 testing, resulting in one transformer damaged beyond repair and a second taken offline for testing. The battery now operates at reduced capacity (350 MW) for SIPS, with full capacity expected in 2026, and the unplanned outage is scheduled until 3 May 2026. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from around 18 October 2025 (when availability dropped to 350 MW) until 3 May 2026 (expected return to full service). Its root_cause is: transformer damage during Hold Point 5 testing, with one transformer beyond repair and potential issues with a second transformer. Its accident scale is: one transformer destroyed, another possibly requiring replacement; battery operating at 350 MW instead of full capacity (unspecified full capacity, but likely ~850 MW based on context). Its dates (if different from the main date) are: event failure occurred around 18 October 2025; outage reported on 7 November 2025; return to service date 3 May 2026. Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (NSW grid). Its affected key users is: NSW electricity grid and SIPS service; Eraring Power Station extension relevance noted. Its cause type is: equipment failure (transformer). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Damage to one transformer beyond repair during Hold Point 5 testing; 2) Issues identified with a second transformer requiring testing; 3) Possible design or manufacturing fault common to all transformers.

title: Sunday evening trip at Callide C4, and wobbles coming back online
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sunday evening trip at Callide C4, and wobbles coming back online" posted at 10-11-2025 09:21:00 AM is: On Sunday 9 November 2025 at 21:26 NEM time, the Callide C4 generator tripped from near full load, causing frequency dips and subsequent instability as the unit attempted to return to service with multiple false starts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (the trip caused frequency wobbles and grid instability, with ongoing impacts during reconnection attempts). Its root_cause is: Unknown (inferred as a generator trip at Callide C4). Its accident scale is: Single unit trip (Callide C4). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 09-11-2025 (event date). Its affected region is: Callide (Queensland, Australia), NEM (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants, grid operators. Its cause type is: Technical failure/generator trip. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: ['Callide C4 tripped from close to full load at 21:26 on 9 Nov 2025.', 'Subsequent false starts and wobbles while attempting to return to service.']

title: A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS from 19th October to 8th November 2025 (with PASA Avail 350MW)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS from 19th October to 8th November 2025 (with PASA Avail 350MW)" posted at 08-11-2025 01:24:00 PM is: The article examines rebids and operational data for the Waratah BESS battery from 19 October to 7 November 2025, noting that after Hold Point 5 testing in mid-October, the unit has been consistently showing a PASA Availability of 350MW (matching the SIPS provision enabled by TransGrid). The author questions what further steps are needed to allow higher capacity operation and references a separate article about an unplanned outage until 3 May 2026, indicating ongoing operational uncertainty. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 19 October 2025 to at least 8 November 2025, with the unplanned outage extending to 3 May 2026. Its root_cause is: Unknown; possibly related to incomplete testing or technical issues preventing full capacity operation beyond 350MW. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no specific accident reported, but an unplanned outage of several months is noted). Its dates is: 08-11-2025 (main date) and the event period 19-10-2025 to 08-11-2025. Its affected region is: Waratah BESS, part of the National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators (TransGrid, AEMO) and possibly consumers relying on system stability services. Its cause type is: Operational/testing issue. Its causes is: Hold Point 5 testing completed in mid-October; subsequent unplanned outage declared; further steps required to enable higher capacity operation; lack of public explanation for the capacity limitation.

title: A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS to 19th October 2025 (during Hold Point 5 testing for full discharge)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at (plant-related) rebids for Waratah BESS to 19th October 2025 (during Hold Point 5 testing for full discharge)" posted at 08-11-2025 12:54:00 PM is: This article examines the rebidding behavior of the Waratah Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) during a 9-day period ending 18 October 2025, when it briefly operated at full discharge and charge capacity as part of Hold Point 5 testing. The analysis, prompted by the earlier discovery of an unplanned outage at the same facility (extending to May 2026), uses ez2view’s Bids & Offers widget to highlight how the battery’s bids and offers changed during the test window. The piece is one of several follow-up articles providing greater detail on the battery’s operations and market interactions. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 9-day period from 10 October to 18 October 2025 (with rebids submitted and effective during that window). Its root_cause is: Hold Point 5 testing of the Waratah BESS to validate full discharge and charge capability. Its accident scale is: Not an accident; planned operational testing of a single large-scale battery (700 MW / 1,400 MWh). Its dates (if different from main date) is: 10-18 October 2025 (the test and rebid period). Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) – the location of the Waratah BESS, which is part of the National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: AEMO (Australian Energy Market Operator), Akaysha Energy (owner/operator), NEM market participants, and grid operators relying on the battery’s services. Its cause type is: Planned commissioning and testing activity. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) The Waratah BESS underwent Hold Point 5 testing to verify its ability to achieve full discharge and charge; 2) As part of this testing, the battery submitted revised bids and offers in the NEM that deviated from its normal pattern; 3) The testing period coincided with the battery briefly reaching its full maximum capacity, prompting the author to examine the corresponding rebid data.

title: Belatedly noting that the AER published its PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist (back on Friday 29th August 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Belatedly noting that the AER published its PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist (back on Friday 29th August 2025)" posted at 07-11-2025 10:26:00 PM is: This article discusses the belated recognition that the Australian Energy Regulator (AER) published its PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist on 29 August 2025, which clarifies how battery operators with System Integrity Protection Scheme (SIPS) contracts should report availability in MT PASA (excluding SIPS reserve) and ST PASA (including SIPS reserve), addressing confusion observed in Waratah BESS outage data. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 29 August 2025 onward (ongoing compliance guidance). Its root_cause is: Need for regulatory clarity on how SIPS reserve capacity should be reflected in PASA availability submissions for batteries, following observed data inconsistencies. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 29-08-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Battery operators (especially those with SIPS contracts), AEMO, market participants. Its cause type is: Regulatory compliance. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) AER published PASA Compliance Bulletin and Checklist on 29 August 2025; 2) Bulletin provides specific guidance on MT PASA (exclude SIPS reserve) vs ST PASA (include SIPS reserve) for batteries; 3) Previous confusion in Waratah BESS outage data prompted this review.

title: An updated longer term trend (and tabulation) of operations at Waratah BESS
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An updated longer term trend (and tabulation) of operations at Waratah BESS" posted at 07-11-2025 09:54:00 PM is: This article provides an updated chronological record and trend analysis of the Waratah BESS (850MW/1,680MWh) in New South Wales, covering operations from August 2025 to 7th November 2025. It highlights a significant unplanned outage flagged on 7th November 2025, reported to last until 3rd May 2026, following earlier peak testing in mid-October 2025 where full discharge (850MW) and charge were achieved. Subsequently, from 19th October 2025, market-based output became more muted with lower availability, and the unit's PASA Availability was reverted to 350MW after Hold Point 5 testing. The article also references related events such as the SIPS (System Integrity Protection Scheme) going live on 1st August 2025, media coverage of record outputs, and the impact of network upgrades for the battery. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 7th November 2025 to 3rd May 2026 (projected outage duration), with prior muted operations from 19th October 2025. Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of the Waratah BESS unit (specific technical cause not disclosed). Its accident scale is: Unknown (significant as it involves a major 850MW battery with a multi-month outage). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-11-2025 (main date), with outage noted from this date. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia (specifically the Central Coast and broader NEM region). Its affected key users is: Grid operators (AEMO, TransGrid), system reliability managers, and electricity consumers in NSW. Its cause type is: Unplanned generator/asset outage (technical failure or maintenance issue). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) Unplanned outage declared for WTAHB1 unit on 7th November 2025, with MT PASA Availability showing outage until 3rd May 2026; 2) Prior reduced output and availability since 19th October 2025 after Hold Point 5 testing ended, with PASA Availability set back to 350MW.

title: Some sizeable cycles in ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Thursday 6th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Some sizeable cycles in ‘Market Demand’ in South Australia on Thursday 6th November 2025" posted at 07-11-2025 04:04:00 PM is: On 6 November 2025, South Australia experienced two distinct cycles of Market Demand between 14:00 and 16:00 NEM time, following a suspected data-glitch triggered significant frequency ramp event earlier in the day; the demand cycles were initially attributed to rooftop PV output changes due to a possible disappearance of cloud cover over Adelaide.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within the day of 6 November 2025).  
Its root_cause is: A combination of a suspected data glitch causing a frequency ramp and possible rapid changes in rooftop PV generation due to cloud cover disappearance.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (documented as "sizeable cycles" but without quantified magnitude).  
Its dates is: 06-11-2025 (same as the event date).  
Its affected region is: South Australia.  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely grid operators and residential/commercial electricity consumers in the region).  
Its cause type is: Operational/technical (data glitch) and weather-related (cloud cover change).  
Its causes is: [1] Suspected data-glitch triggered frequency ramp event earlier in the day; [2] Possible sudden disappearance of cloud cover affecting rooftop PV output around Adelaide.

title: What’s up with Waratah BESS (unplanned outage till 3rd May 2026)?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "What’s up with Waratah BESS (unplanned outage till 3rd May 2026)?" posted at 07-11-2025 02:53:00 PM is: On November 7, 2025, AEMO's MT PASA DUID Availability data revealed an unplanned outage for the Waratah BESS (unit WTAHB1) that suddenly extended to May 3, 2026, despite near-term bids and rebids showing the battery currently operational and available. The reason for this lengthy outage is unknown, though the article speculates it could be related to the SIPS (System Integrity Protection Scheme). The loss of up to 850 MW of battery capacity for about six months could significantly affect grid stability and electricity market prices in the NEM, particularly in New South Wales. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Turbine/generator failures (battery storage unit outage). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 07-11-2025 (outage start/extension) to 03-05-2026 (scheduled return). Its root_cause is: Unknown (possibly SIPS-related). Its accident scale is: Loss of up to 850 MW (MaxCap) of battery availability for ~6 months. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 07-11-2025 (news date) to 03-05-2026 (outage end). Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly New South Wales (location of Waratah BESS). Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers and market participants in the NEM, especially those relying on battery storage for grid support. Its cause type is: Unplanned outage (sudden extension in MT PASA data). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Unknown cause; possible SIPS (System Integrity Protection Scheme) involvement; no official explanation from Akaysha Energy or AEMO as of the article date].

title: Mainland Frequency wanders south early Friday afternoon 7th November 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Mainland Frequency wanders south early Friday afternoon 7th November 2025" posted at 07-11-2025 02:03:00 PM is: On Friday 7 November 2025 at 13:56 NEM time, the mainland grid frequency was observed wandering south, indicating a deviation from the nominal 50 Hz. This event signals a brief period of grid instability, likely stemming from an imbalance between supply and demand, though the specific root cause is not detailed in the report.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate, short-term frequency deviation event; Its root_cause is: Unknown; Its accident scale is: Minor frequency deviation (no reported blackout or significant disruption); Its dates is: 07-11-2025; Its affected region is: Mainland NEM (National Electricity Market, likely eastern Australia); Its affected key users is: All grid-connected consumers and generators; Its cause type is: Unknown; Its causes is: [Unknown, no specific causal events identified].

title: (First ever?) forecast MSL warnings issued for South Australia for the coming week
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "(First ever?) forecast MSL warnings issued for South Australia for the coming week" posted at 07-11-2025 12:37:00 PM is: On 7 November 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued multiple Minimum System Load (MSL) warnings for South Australia for 8, 9, 11, and 12 November 2025, forecasting demand to fall below MSL1 and, on 11 November, below MSL2 thresholds. These conditions indicate an elevated risk of insufficient demand to maintain a secure operating state, highlighting a grid/system instability issue potentially linked to high renewable generation or low consumption. This is believed to be the first time such MSL warnings have been issued for South Australia.

Its relevance level is: Level 1  
Its category is: grid/system instability  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 8-12 November 2025  
Its root_cause is: Forecast low demand threatening system security  
Its accident scale is: Forecast MSL1 and MSL2 conditions  
Its dates (if different) are: 8, 9, 11, 12 November 2025  
Its affected region is: South Australia  
Its affected key users is: AEMO and National Electricity Market participants  
Its cause type is: Forecast low demand  
Its causes is: AEMO published market notices MN130458, MN130461, MN130459, MN130462, and MN130460 warning of MSL1 (and MSL2 on 11 Nov) conditions in SA.

title: A quick look at ‘Dispatch Error’ on Thursday 6th November 2025, in conjunction with the Frequency Spike
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at ‘Dispatch Error’ on Thursday 6th November 2025, in conjunction with the Frequency Spike" posted at 07-11-2025 10:39:00 AM is: On Thursday 6th November 2025, a significant frequency spike peaking at 50.229Hz occurred in the National Electricity Market (NEM), caused by a data glitch that added approximately 1,700 MW of 'phantom demand' in Victoria. This erroneous input led the market dispatch engine (NEMDE) to dispatch units to meet the nonexistent demand, causing frequency to exceed the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB). The article analyzes 'Dispatch Error' (deviation from target) across fuel types, noting that scheduled units like coal and batteries intentionally reduced output to help rein in frequency, while semi-scheduled units (solar, wind) showed mixed responses. The peak was reached at 12:45:21.2, and frequency returned to normal after the phantom demand was removed in the subsequent dispatch interval. The incident highlights operational risks from data errors and grid instability. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, specifically on 6th November 2025 during the lunchtime period. Its root_cause is: Data glitch that added phantom demand. Its accident scale is: ~1,700 MW of phantom demand leading to a frequency deviation to 50.229 Hz. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 06-11-2025 (event date), 07-11-2025 (analysis date). Its affected region is: Victoria and the broader National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: AEMO, market participants (generators), and system operators. Its cause type is: Operational/data error. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) A data glitch added about 1,700 MW of phantom demand in Victoria; 2) NEMDE dispatched units to meet the erroneous demand; 3) The mismatch caused frequency to spike to 50.229 Hz, exceeding the NOFB.

title: A quick look at some of the market dynamics around the NEM during this afternoon’s frequency episode
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "A quick look at some of the market dynamics around the NEM during this afternoon’s frequency episode" posted at 06-11-2025 03:45:00 PM is: Earlier today, a mainland frequency spike occurred in the NEM just after 12:20pm, attributed to an AEMO-reported demand rise in Victoria that may have been a data glitch, leading to an over-frequency event. This prompted additional generation dispatch from NSW and Queensland, with interconnector flows shifting southward and solar farms in QLD, NSW, and VIC briefly spiking output, though no significant spot price changes were observed. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, within the 12:15 to 13:15 dispatch intervals on 06-11-2025. Its root_cause is: A reported demand spike in Victoria (likely an AEMO data glitch) that caused the dispatch engine to allocate more generation than needed, resulting in an over-frequency condition. Its accident scale is: Unknown (brief frequency event, no major outages reported). Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 06-11-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly Victoria, with impacts on NSW, Queensland, and South Australia.
