title: Was that a data glitch that saw lunchtime ‘Market Demand’ ramp in Victoria by 1,812MW?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Was that a data glitch that saw lunchtime ‘Market Demand’ ramp in Victoria by 1,812MW?" posted at 06-11-2025 01:39:00 PM is: A data glitch caused NEMDE to incorrectly register a steep increase in Victoria's Market Demand, ramping it by 1,812 MW over 20 minutes (from 2,734 MW at 12:25 to 4,546 MW at 12:45) and then dropping back sharply at 12:50. This phantom demand led to higher generation targets, spiking system frequency until corrective action was taken. No market notices were issued by AEMO.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).  
Its category is: grid/system instability.  
Its timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (minutes, on 06-11-2025).  
Its root_cause is: A data glitch in NEMDE that falsely indicated a large demand increase.  
Its accident scale is: Medium (significant 1,812 MW ramp but isolated to data error, no physical damage).  
Its dates is: 06-11-2025.  
Its affected region is: Victoria (NEM region).  
Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely AEMO and scheduled/semi-scheduled generators).  
Its cause type is: Technical/data glitch.  
Its causes is: A data glitch causing NEMDE to misinterpret grid demand; subsequent over-targeting of generation units; resulting frequency overshoot; corrective removal of phantom demand.

title: Large under-performance across Solar Farms as the main driver of frequency wobbles on Thursday afternoon 30th October 2025?
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Large under-performance across Solar Farms as the main driver of frequency wobbles on Thursday afternoon 30th October 2025?" posted at 03-11-2025 09:55:00 AM is: On Thursday 30 October 2025 at approximately 17:00, a significant frequency drop to 49.84 Hz occurred in the National Electricity Market (NEM), driven primarily by a large collective under-performance of solar farms (aggregate Dispatch Error of +613 MW) compounded by wind farm under-performance (+270 MW), totaling +883 MW from semi-scheduled units. BESS units (-480 MW) and coal units (-242 MW) partially offset the deviation, but the event highlighted issues with renewable forecastability and the scalability of the semi-scheduled category, with particular large deviations at Western Downs solar farm (+153 MW), Wellington North solar farm (+97 MW), and Clark Creek 1 wind farm (+287 MW), possibly storm-related. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the 17:00 dispatch interval on 30 October 2025). Its root_cause is: Large under-performance of solar and wind farms due to variability and forecasting errors, exacerbated by storm season. Its accident scale is: Aggregate dispatch error of +883 MW from semi-scheduled units; frequency drop to 49.84 Hz (just outside normal operating frequency band). Its dates is: 30-10-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Grid operators, market participants, and system operators. Its cause type is: Operational/forecasting issue (variability of renewable generation). Its causes is: (1) Large collective under-performance across solar farms NEM-wide (+613 MW); (2) Notable collective under-performance across wind farms (+270 MW); (3) Specific large deviations at WDGPH1 (+153 MW), WELNSF1 (+97 MW), and CLRKCWF1 (+287 MW); (4) Use of persistence forecasts by solar farms contributing to under-performance; (5) Potential storm-related weather impacts.

title: Comparing AggROT for (44x) Coal Units with AggROT for Semi-Scheduled units through the history of the NEM
author: Paul McArdle
topic: Is the Semi-Scheduled Category Sustainable or Scalable?
classified_content: The summary of the news "Comparing AggROT for (44x) Coal Units with AggROT for Semi-Scheduled units through the history of the NEM" posted at 29-10-2025 09:06:00 AM is: This article analyzes the Aggregate Raw Off-Target (AggROT) metric over more than 20 years for 44 coal units and ~10 years for the growing fleet of semi-scheduled units in Australia's National Electricity Market (NEM). It highlights a stark divergence: while coal units' collective under-performance remains consistent, semi-scheduled units have experienced a rapid escalation in large collective under-performance (AggROT > +500MW) beginning in 2021, accelerating in 2022, pausing in 2023-2024, and resuming sharply in 2025 (through Q3). Specific incidents in 2025 (e.g., 31 Jan, 15 Feb, 31 Mar, 19 Apr, 19 Aug) show semi-scheduled under-performance often exceeding 800MW, causing frequency disturbances that coal units then attempt to correct via FCAS and PFR. The article argues that the semi-scheduled category's looser dispatch control and increasing penetration of variable renewable energy (VRE) create scalability and sustainability concerns, as diversity does not compensate for simultaneous under-performance. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: ongoing, with trend from 2021 to 2025 Q3, and specific incidents in 2025. Its root_cause is: The semi-scheduled category’s much looser dispatch control compared to fully scheduled units, combined with resource intermittency and the inability of spatial diversity to offset simultaneous under-performance, leading to large collective off-target deviations that strain frequency control. Its accident scale is: Multiple large incidents; the largest observed is AggROT of +1,678MW over-performance (19 Aug 2025) and under-performance up to +944MW; total of 6 dispatch intervals >500MW under-performance on 15 Feb 2025, and numerous others in 2025. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 31 Jan 2025, 9 Feb 2025, 15 Feb 2025, 31 Mar 2025, 19 Apr 2025, 19 Aug 2025, 14 Sep 2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) of Australia. Its affected key users are: AEMO, coal-fired power stations, semi-scheduled wind and solar farms, and market participants relying on system frequency stability. Its cause type is: Operational and regulatory (design of semi-scheduled category). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Looser dispatch obligations for semi-scheduled units versus fully scheduled units; 2) Increasing penetration of variable renewable energy (wind and solar) with intermittent output; 3) Simultaneous under-performance of multiple semi-scheduled units without counterbalancing over-performance from other semi-scheduled units; 4) Inadequate forecasting and self-forecast errors (noted for 19 Aug 2025); 5) Nelson Review panel equating semi-scheduled and fully scheduled categories despite critical differences in predictability.

A rebid was submitted shortly after with the reason 'electrical', and the unit returned to service overnight. No further official statement was issued by CS Energy. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate short-term impact (within hours). Its root_cause is: electrical issue (unknown specific cause). Its accident scale is: single generator unit trip. Its dates is: 28-10-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (NEM). Its affected key users is: NEM grid participants. Its cause type is: Electrical fault/trip. Its causes is: Trip of Callide B2 unit from almost full load; rebid reason 'electrical'.

title: On 27th October 2025, AEMO declared Scheduling Error #73 (with respect to 19th August 2025)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "On 27th October 2025, AEMO declared Scheduling Error #73 (with respect to 19th August 2025)" posted at 27-10-2025 08:00:00 PM is: On 19 August 2025, a software update by a self-forecast provider incorrectly set the Unconstrained Intermittent Generation Forecast (UIGF) for 34 semi-scheduled plants to zero, causing a generation surplus of approximately 1,550 MW. This surplus pushed the mainland frequency above the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB) upper limit of 50.15 Hz. The Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) later declared a Scheduling Error (Incident #73) on 27 October 2025, noting the incident duration was 30 minutes (11:45 to 12:15). AEMO rectified the issue by suppressing the self-forecast provider until the risk was mitigated.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 19 August 2025, 11:45 to 12:15 (30 minutes). Its root_cause is: A self-forecast provider software update incorrectly set UIGF for 34 semi-scheduled plants to zero. Its accident scale is: 1,550 MW generation surplus causing frequency excursion beyond the NOFB upper limit (50.15 Hz). Its dates is: 19-08-2025 (event date); news posted 27-10-2025. Its affected region is: Australian mainland (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: All grid-connected electricity consumers (frequency deviation impacts all users). Its cause type is: IT/software error. Its causes is: 1) Self-forecast provider software update incorrectly set UIGF for 34 semi-scheduled plants to zero; 2) This led to a generation surplus of 1,550 MW; 3) The surplus caused mainland frequency to exceed the NOFB upper limit.

title: Sharp mainland frequency drop, with trip of CPP_4 on Sunday evening 26th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Sharp mainland frequency drop, with trip of CPP_4 on Sunday evening 26th October 2025" posted at 26-10-2025 06:51:00 PM is: On Sunday 26 October 2025, a sharp drop in mainland frequency occurred around 18:30 NEM time, followed by a notification that the Callide C4 unit (CPP_4) tripped from 420 MW during the 5-minute period ending at 18:36. CS Energy later confirmed that the unit tripped at approximately 6:30 pm due to a storm and lightning strike on Powerlink Queensland's Calvale switchyard, which connects the plant to the power grid. The unit is forecast to return to service by Friday 31 October 2025 at 6 pm. Additional context: Callide units B1 and B2 were online, while unit C3 remains offline for a major overhaul until mid-November. Storm damage to the C3 and C4 cooling towers was reported as non-structural.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 26 October 2025 18:30 to approximately 31 October 2025 18:00 (about 5 days). Its root_cause is: storm and lightning strike to Powerlink Queensland's Calvale switchyard, causing the trip of Callide C4. Its accident scale is: One 420 MW unit tripped; non-structural damage to cooling towers. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 26-10-2025. Its affected region is: Queensland, Australia (specifically Callide Power Station and the grid interconnected via Calvale switchyard). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: External event (storm/lightning). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: [Storm; Lightning strike to Calvale switchyard; Trip of Callide C4 generator].

title: Two *big concerns* that we urge the Nelson Review panel to (re)consider (with respect to Invisible Responses) following review of the Draft Report
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two *big concerns* that we urge the Nelson Review panel to (re)consider (with respect to Invisible Responses) following review of the Draft Report" posted at 26-10-2025 05:00:00 PM is: The article highlights two major concerns with the draft Nelson Review report on NEM reforms: first, a "whiplash" from encouraging voluntary participation of price-responsive resources to recommending mandatory visibility/dispatchability by 2030; second, the report’s neglect of the escalating frequency instability caused by semi-scheduled (intermittent) renewable resources, which the author argues is a growing elephant-in-the-room issue leading to grid instability and economic impacts. The piece calls for the panel to reconsider these blind spots, especially given the rising frequency of AggROT and frequency exceedances. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing and escalating (2024–2025 onward), with proposed mandate by 2030. Its root_cause is: Increasing penetration of semi-scheduled (intermittent) renewable resources causing frequency control challenges, and a proposed policy shift from voluntary to mandatory participation. Its accident scale is: Multiple frequency exceedances and grid instability events catalogued in recent years. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) are: 7–8 October 2025 (NEMdev conference), draft report published earlier in 2025, final report expected late November/end of 2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users are: AEMO, generators, and electricity consumers. Its cause type is: Technical (grid instability) and regulatory/policy. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1. Rise of semi-scheduled resources (wind, solar) reducing visibility and scheduling; 2. Increasing frequency of frequency and AggROT exceedances; 3. Draft report's Recommendation 2B mandating participation by 2030 (Concern #1); 4. Oversight of semi-scheduled category issues and their impact on frequency stability (Concern #2).

title: Selected 4-second data relating to the (SemiSched-induced) frequency weakness on Thursday 16th October 2025 (i.e. Part 4)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Selected 4-second data relating to the (SemiSched-induced) frequency weakness on Thursday 16th October 2025 (i.e. Part 4)" posted at 25-10-2025 09:15:00 PM is: On 16 October 2025, a significant frequency weakness event occurred in the Australian NEM, where the frequency dropped to 49.824 Hz (below the Normal Operating Frequency Band) due to large positive dispatch errors from semi-scheduled wind and solar farms. The most severe incident was at the 16:50 dispatch interval, where solar farms in NSW and SA experienced unforeseen cloud cover causing rapid output drops (aggregate +422 MW harmful deviations), while wind farms in SA and Victoria faced technical challenges ramping up from zero after being constrained off (also +422 MW aggregate harmful). These were partially offset by BESS (-465 MW) and coal (-329 MW). The analysis used 4-second SCADA data to highlight specific unit patterns, raising concerns about the sustainability of semi-scheduled generation under current rules. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Afternoon of 16 October 2025, with the most critical impact at 16:50 dispatch interval. Its root_cause is: Collective underperformance of semi-scheduled wind and solar farms due to unforeseen cloud cover (solar) and physical ramp limitations (wind), causing large positive dispatch errors that dragged frequency below NOFB. Its accident scale is: Frequency dropped to 49.824 Hz, outside NOFB; multiple large deviations (≥40 MW) from 6 solar and 6 wind units; aggregate errors of +422 MW each for solar and wind at 16:50. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-10-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) – specifically New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria, and Queensland. Its affected key users is: All electricity users in the NEM due to system frequency instability. Its cause type is: Weather (cloud cover) and technical/operational (wind farm ramp constraints, forecasting errors). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Unforeseen fast-moving cloud cover over solar farms in NSW and SA; physical limitations in older wind farms preventing rapid ramp-up from zero after curtailment; self-forecast errors by several solar farms failing to anticipate cloud cover; AEMO's ASEFS system not predicting the cloud effect.

title: A hot afternoon in NSW on Wednesday 22nd October 2025 (but the price did not pop … at least to 14:15)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "A hot afternoon in NSW on Wednesday 22nd October 2025 (but the price did not pop … at least to 14:15)" posted at 22-10-2025 03:24:00 PM is: On 22 October 2025, a hot afternoon in New South Wales increased electricity demand, but prices did not spike significantly due to a transmission line outage on the Dapto-Marulan 330kV line that triggered constraint equations, reducing semi-scheduled variable renewable energy generation; the outage ended before the evening peak, preventing further market impact. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (during the day until 14:15 NEM time on 22 October 2025). Its root_cause is: outage on the Dapto-Marulan 330kV line causing binding constraint equations that limited VRE output. Its accident scale is: single transmission line outage. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 22-10-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (specifically the Dapto-Marulan area and broader NEM region). Its affected key users is: semi-scheduled variable renewable energy (VRE) generators (wind and solar) constrained down by the constraint set. Its cause type is: transmission line outage (unplanned or planned not specified). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: ["Outage on the Dapto-Marulan 330kV line", "High temperatures increasing electricity demand in NSW"].

title: WeatherWatch notes ‘Cyclonic Conditions for Victoria, Widespread Gales Across NSW & Areas of South Australia’
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "WeatherWatch notes ‘Cyclonic Conditions for Victoria, Widespread Gales Across NSW & Areas of South Australia’" posted at 22-10-2025 08:22:00 AM is: On 22 October 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued multiple market notices reclassifying non‑credible contingency events as credible due to severe weather (cyclonic conditions and widespread gales) forecast for Victoria, South Australia, and New South Wales. Specifically, AEMO reclassified several transmission line trips in South Australia (Para‑Templers West, Magill‑Torrens Island A, Brinkworth‑Davenport, Brinkworth‑Templers West) and a general widespread abnormal condition for Victoria, invoking constraint set I‑VS_250 to maintain power system security. The notices took effect from 07:45 hrs (SA) and 08:00 hrs (Vic) on 22 October until further notice.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Extreme weather. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: From 0745 hrs 22/10/2025 until further notice. Its root_cause is: Severe weather (cyclonic conditions, widespread gales) increasing the likelihood of transmission line trips. Its accident scale is: Multiple transmission line contingency reclassifications (at least 4 specific lines in SA and a region‑wide reclassification for Vic). Its dates is: 22-10-2025. Its affected region is: South Australia and Victoria. Its affected key users is: NEM participants, electricity consumers in SA and Vic. Its cause type is: Weather‑related (extreme weather). Its causes is: Cyclonic conditions in Victoria, widespread gales across NSW and South Australia.

title: Taking a first focused look at the 16:50 dispatch interval on Thursday 16th October 2025 (with frequency dropped below NOFB) … Part 3
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Taking a first focused look at the 16:50 dispatch interval on Thursday 16th October 2025 (with frequency dropped below NOFB) … Part 3" posted at 20-10-2025 07:35:00 PM is: On Thursday 16th October 2025 at the 16:50 dispatch interval, system frequency fell to 49.824 Hz (outside the Normal Operating Frequency Band) due to a large aggregate dispatch error of +844 MW across semi-scheduled solar and wind farms. Solar farms (mainly in NSW and SA) underperformed because of fast-moving cloud cover not caught by forecasts, while wind farms (mostly in SA and VIC) struggled with technical ramping issues after being economically curtailed in the prior interval, collectively dragging frequency down. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16th October 2025. Its root_cause is: large aggregate dispatch error of +844MW from semi-scheduled solar and wind farms due to weather effects (cloud cover) and technical ramping issues. Its accident scale is: frequency drop to 49.824 Hz, outside NOFB, with aggregate dispatch error of +844MW. Its dates is: 16-10-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM) in Australia, particularly New South Wales, South Australia, Victoria, and Queensland. Its affected key users is: grid system operators and all electricity consumers reliant on stable frequency. Its cause type is: operational and technical. Its causes is: (1) Solar farms: unexpected output reduction due to fast-moving cloud cover not forecasted; (2) Wind farms: technical difficulty in ramping up from zero output after economic curtailment in prior dispatch interval.

title: Unplanned outage of Dapto – Marulan (8) 330 kV line
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Unplanned outage of Dapto – Marulan (8) 330 kV line" posted at 20-10-2025 12:59:00 PM is: The AEMO flagged an unplanned outage of the Dapto–Marulan (8) 330 kV line at 07:55 on 20 October 2025, invoking the N-DTMN_8_15M constraint set at 08:00, which affected inter-regional transfer limits on the NSW–QLD, VIC–NSW, and other interconnectors. This event coincided with a spike in price forecasts for NSW earlier in the day, though forecasts moderated by the 11:30 P30 predispatch run. The outage is expected to be resolved by 17:00 NEM time on the same day. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 07:55 to 17:00 on 20-10-2025 (same-day, short-term). Its root_cause is: Unplanned outage of a 330 kV transmission line (exact cause not specified). Its accident scale is: Minor (single transmission line). Its dates are: 20-10-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) region, specifically the Dapto–Marulan corridor; interconnectors to Queensland and Victoria also impacted. Its affected key users is: Unknown (general generators and consumers in NSW and interconnected regions). Its cause type is: Unknown (unplanned outage, possibly equipment failure). Its causes are: Unplanned outage of Dapto–Marulan (8) 330 kV line.

title: Spicy prices forecast for NSW on Monday afternoon/evening 20th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Spicy prices forecast for NSW on Monday afternoon/evening 20th October 2025" posted at 20-10-2025 11:00:00 AM is: AEMO issued market notices on 20 October 2025 forecasting high electricity prices near the Market Price Cap in New South Wales for a 90-120 minute period that afternoon/evening, due to steady demand around 11,500 MW and a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition. AEMO identified a foreseeable reliability circumstance requiring potential intervention from 16:30 hrs, including a direction to generators if market response is insufficient by 16:05 hrs. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: immediate, starting 16:30 hrs on 20 October 2025. Its root_cause is: forecast high demand combined with insufficient reserve margin (LOR2) triggering reliability concerns. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 20-10-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW), Australia. Its affected key users is: electricity consumers and market participants in NSW. Its cause type is: operational/forecast-driven. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: forecast market demand of ~11,500 MW; steady LOR2 warning; AEMO intervention notice for reliability.

title: Forecast Minimum System Load for Victoria next Saturday 25th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Forecast Minimum System Load for Victoria next Saturday 25th October 2025" posted at 19-10-2025 02:23:00 PM is: AEMO issued a market notice on 19 October 2025 forecasting a Minimum System Load (MSL1) condition in the Victoria (VIC) region for 25 October 2025, indicating an elevated risk of insufficient demand (forecast to drop to 1695 MW, below the MSL1 threshold of 1720 MW) between 1230 and 1300 hrs, which could compromise grid security. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: specific future date 25 October 2025, during the forecast period of 12:30–13:00 hrs. Its root_cause is: forecast operational demand falling below the MSL1 threshold due to low electricity consumption. Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident, only a forecast condition). Its dates (if different from the main date) is: same as main date – event date 25 October 2025, notice date 19 October 2025. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC), Australia (National Electricity Market). Its affected key users is: Unknown (likely all electricity consumers and market participants in VIC). Its cause type is: Operational/demand-side (low demand). Its causes is: [Forecast low demand (1695 MW) below the MSL1 threshold (1720 MW) for a brief period on 25 October 2025].

title: Considerable uncertainty, with respect to what might unfold in NSW next Wednesday 22nd October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Considerable uncertainty, with respect to what might unfold in NSW next Wednesday 22nd October 2025" posted at 18-10-2025 08:35:00 AM is: AEMO issued a market notice updating a forecast Lack of Reserve Level 2 (LOR2) condition for the NSW region on 22 October 2025, from 15:30 to 18:00, with a capacity reserve requirement of 700 MW but only 496 MW available. The article highlights significant uncertainty in market demand forecasts, leading to changing reserve conditions, including a previous forecast of LOR3 (load shedding) that was later revised downward. The situation remains under watch. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 22 October 2025, from 15:30 to 18:00. Its root_cause is: Forecast supply-demand imbalance due to uncertainty in market demand. Its accident scale is: LOR2 condition, reserve shortfall of 204 MW (700 MW required minus 496 MW available). Its dates is: 22 October 2025. Its affected region is: NSW. Its affected key users is: All electricity consumers in NSW (load shedding risk). Its cause type is: Forecast uncertainty (operational). Its causes is: Uncertainty in market demand forecasts; possible other uncertainties not checked.

title: Price spike in NSW (and QLD!) at 14:55 on Friday 17th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Price spike in NSW (and QLD!) at 14:55 on Friday 17th October 2025" posted at 17-10-2025 04:27:00 PM is: The article reports a significant energy price spike in NSW at 14:55 on 17 October 2025, with elevated contingency FCAS prices in QLD, driven by binding constraint equations from outages on the Avon to Marulan 330kV line and the Armidale to Dumaresq line. These constraints caused counter-priced flows on the VIC1-NSW1 and QNI interconnectors, creating an economic island for NSW, and forced the RYEPARK1 generator to reduce output by 212 MW. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within the dispatch interval at 14:55 on 17 October 2025). Its root_cause is: Outages on the Avon to Marulan 330kV line and the Armidale to Dumaresq line causing binding constraint equations. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 17-10-2025. Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW) and Queensland (QLD). Its affected key users is: Generators and consumers in NSW, particularly the RYEPARK1 generator. Its cause type is: Transmission line outages. Its causes is: Outage on Avon to Marulan (16) 330kV line, Outage on Armidale to Dumaresq (8C) line.

title: How the NEM BESS fleet is operating on peak days, and how to be a savvy operator
author: Matt Grover
classified_content: The summary of the news "How the NEM BESS fleet is operating on peak days, and how to be a savvy operator" posted at 17-10-2025 11:40:00 AM is: This article, adapted from a presentation by Fluence’s Matt Grover, analyzes how the National Electricity Market’s Battery Energy Storage Systems operated during three peak days in June 2025 (12th, 18th, 26th). It reveals that while most BESS discharged fully during early price spikes (e.g., $10,000/MWh), few captured the highest prices ($17,500/MWh) due to price elasticity—the fleet itself suppressed prices until energy ran out. Human traders increasingly influenced outcomes, applying lessons from previous days (e.g., holding back on June 18th, stretching duration on June 26th), and the article emphasizes that simplistic “percent of perfect” metrics misrepresent performance because perfect revenue capture is unrealistic. Key takeaways include the diversity of strategies creating an aggregate long-duration effect, the importance of flexible bidding systems, good forecasting, and post-event analytics for savvy trading. Its relevance level is: Level 2 (Relevant). Its category is: Market reactions. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (specific peak days in June 2025). Its root_cause is: High demand and tight supply leading to price spikes, with BESS fleet behavior influencing price formation through elasticity. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) are: 12-06-2025, 18-06-2025, 26-06-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), with a focus on Victoria. Its affected key users is: BESS operators, market participants, and traders. Its cause type is: Human decision-making and algorithmic trading strategies. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Price elasticity effects; BESS fleet suppressing early peak prices; traders applying lessons from prior days; ‘duration stretching’ as a risk management technique; varying risk appetites across fleet.

title: Peeling the second layer of the onion, with respect to the (SemiSched-induced) frequency weakness on Thursday 16th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Peeling the second layer of the onion, with respect to the (SemiSched-induced) frequency weakness on Thursday 16th October 2025" posted at 17-10-2025 10:23:00 AM is: The article details frequency stability weaknesses in the National Electricity Market (NEM) on Thursday 16th October 2025, caused by sustained underperformance (large positive dispatch errors) from semi-scheduled solar and wind farms throughout the afternoon. Four critical dispatch intervals are examined: at 13:35 a frequency drop to 49.909 Hz following trips of YWPS3 and YWPS4; at 15:10 a drop to 49.865 Hz with combined solar and wind dispatch errors of +593 MW and +272 MW; at 15:25 an overshoot to 50.088 Hz; and at 16:50 a drop to 49.824 Hz (outside the normal operating frequency band) with solar and wind each at +422 MW. Frequency was stabilised by coal units, battery energy storage systems (BESS), and minor contributions from hydro and gas, but the article questions the scalability of the semi-scheduled category.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16th October 2025 (afternoon). Its root_cause is: Systemic underperformance of semi-scheduled solar and wind farms leading to large positive dispatch errors, combined with generator trips (YWPS3 and YWPS4). Its accident scale is: Multiple frequency disturbances across four dispatch intervals, with the lowest frequency at 49.824 Hz (outside NOFB). Its dates is: 16-10-2025. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and generation performance issue / Semi-scheduled VRE underperformance. Its causes is: Underperformance of semi-scheduled solar farms (large positive dispatch errors), underperformance of semi-scheduled wind farms (large positive dispatch errors), trips of YWPS3 and YWPS4 coal units.

title: Aldoga Solar Farm on Sunday 12th October 2025 – Part 2
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aldoga Solar Farm on Sunday 12th October 2025 – Part 2" posted at 17-10-2025 09:46:00 AM is: On 12 October 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) declared Aldoga Solar Farm non-conforming over two bouts because the unit continued generating despite being economically curtailed and under a semi-dispatch cap, causing an oversupply of up to 337 MW relative to its target. This led to a frequency spike on the mainland grid that remained within the Normal Operating Frequency Band, with corrective responses primarily from batteries (notably Hazelwood BESS) and other units via primary frequency response and regulation FCAS. The deviations incurred a total cost of $1,095 over a 35-minute period, highlighting potential high costs if regulation prices spike. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (within the day of 12 October 2025, specifically the 09:50–10:20 period). Its root_cause is: The solar farm continued generating despite being economically curtailed and under a semi-dispatch cap, leading to oversupply and non-conformance. Its accident scale is: 337 MW deviation; $1,095 cost; frequency spike remained within NOFB. Its dates is: 12 October 2025 (event date; news posted 17 October 2025). Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM) mainland grid. Its affected key users is: AEMO, market participants (generators and battery operators). Its cause type is: Operational/technical non-conformance. Its causes is: 1) Aldoga Solar Farm's energy bid was priced out yet the unit continued to generate; 2) A semi-dispatch cap was in place, making the oversupply relative to zero target cause non-conformance; 3) The oversupply led to a frequency rise that required corrective responses from batteries and other units.

title: Weakness in frequency stability on Thursday afternoon 16th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Weakness in frequency stability on Thursday afternoon 16th October 2025" posted at 17-10-2025 08:20:00 AM is: The article reports a weakness in frequency stability on the afternoon of 16 October 2025, initially linked to simultaneous trips at YWPS3 and YWPS4, but further analysis indicates those trips were not the primary drivers; instead, the worst frequency disruptions were caused by challenges in forecasting and dispatching variable renewable energy (VRE) later in the day. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (intraday, 16 October 2025 afternoon). Its root_cause is: Challenges in forecasting and dispatching variable renewable energy (VRE) leading to frequency instability. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 16 October 2025. Its affected region is: Unknown. Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Operational and forecasting issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Simultaneous trips at YWPS3 and YWPS4 (not primary); 2. Inaccurate VRE forecasting and dispatch later in the day.

title: Both YWPS3 and YWPS4 back online overnight
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Both YWPS3 and YWPS4 back online overnight" posted at 17-10-2025 07:39:00 AM is: YWPS3 and YWPS4, two generating units at a power station (likely Yallourn in Victoria), tripped simultaneously on Thursday 16th October 2025, causing temporary outages, but both units returned to service overnight and were back online by Friday morning 17th October 2025.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, from 16th to 17th October 2025 (less than 24 hours). Its root_cause is: Simultaneous trips of YWPS3 and YWPS4; specific root cause unknown. Its accident scale is: Two generation units tripped offline. Its dates is: Event occurred on 16-10-2025, units returned by 17-10-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (likely Yallourn Power Station). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Technical failure (likely). Its causes is: Simultaneous trips of YWPS3 and YWPS4.

title: Differing return to service expectations for YWPS3 and YWPS4, following simultaneous trips
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Differing return to service expectations for YWPS3 and YWPS4, following simultaneous trips" posted at 16-10-2025 04:09:00 PM is: On 16 October 2025, simultaneous trips occurred at Yallourn W Power Station units 3 and 4 (YWPS3 and YWPS4) in Victoria, causing system strength and frequency disturbances. Return to service expectations differ: YWPS3 expected later that evening, YWPS4 expected midday on 17 October 2025; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 16-10-2025 to 17-10-2025. Its root_cause is: Simultaneous trips of two generating units at Yallourn W power station. Its accident scale is: Two units (YWPS3 and YWPS4). Its dates is: 16-10-2025 to 17-10-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria (Australia). Its affected key users is: Unknown. Its cause type is: Generator trip. Its causes is: Simultaneous trips of YWPS3 and YWPS4; Other units already out including LYA4.

title: System frequency during simultaneous trips at YWPS3 and YWPS4 on Thursday 16th October 2025
author: Linton Corbet
classified_content: The summary of the news "System frequency during simultaneous trips at YWPS3 and YWPS4 on Thursday 16th October 2025" posted at 16-10-2025 03:41:00 PM is: On Thursday 16 October 2025, simultaneous trips at YWPS3 and YWPS4 caused a coincident loss of nearly 600 MW of supply, leading to a frequency drop that reached 49.9 Hz but remained within the Normal Operating Frequency Band (NOFB). title: After Yallourn units trip, AEMO again issues directions to Jeeralang Power Station, for System Strength in Victoria
author: Adam Kent
classified_content:
The summary of the news "After Yallourn units trip, AEMO again issues directions to Jeeralang Power Station, for System Strength in Victoria" posted at 16-10-2025 03:33:00 PM is: On 16 October 2025, following the simultaneous trip of units YWPS3 and YWPS4 at the Yallourn power station, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) issued directions to multiple units at the Jeeralang Power Station (JLA02, JLA03, JLB03) to synchronize and follow dispatch targets in order to maintain system strength in the Victoria region. The directions were issued under clause 4.8.9(a1)(1) and declared an AEMO intervention event from 1355 hrs on the same day, with no intervention pricing applied. The event highlights the impact of generator failures on grid stability.  

Its relevance level is: Level 1.  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: short-term (immediate and ongoing until further notice).  
Its root_cause is: simultaneous trip of Yallourn units YWPS3 and YWPS4.  
Its accident scale is: two generating units tripped.  
Its dates is: 16-10-2025 (same as main date).  
Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia.  
Its affected key users is: AEMO, EnergyAustralia Ecogen Pty Ltd (operator of Jeeralang), and Victoria electricity consumers.  
Its cause type is: forced outage (unplanned generator trip).  
Its causes is: [simultaneous trip of Yallourn units YWPS3 and YWPS4; resulting system strength deficiency requiring AEMO direction to synchronize Jeeralang units].

title: Simultaneous trips at YWPS3 and YWPS4 on Thursday 16th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Simultaneous trips at YWPS3 and YWPS4 on Thursday 16th October 2025" posted at 16-10-2025 02:44:00 PM is: On 16 October 2025 at 13:35 NEM time, Yallourn Power Station Units 3 and 4 tripped simultaneously, resulting in a coincident loss of approximately 600 MW of generation. The event caused a frequency disruption on the mainland grid and triggered a non-credible contingency market notice (ID 129724) from AEMO, which confirmed no load shedding was instructed. Wholesale electricity prices trended upward following the trip. The root cause is unknown but suspected to involve a substation incident. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate; event at 13:35 on 16-10-2025, with price effects observed shortly after. Its root_cause is: Unknown; suspected substation-related common cause. Its accident scale is: Loss of approximately 600 MW generation capacity. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-10-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia (VIC region). Its affected key users is: Yallourn Power Station (Units 3 and 4), AEMO, NEM market participants. Its cause type is: Non-credible contingency event (simultaneous trip of two generating units). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Simultaneous trip of Yallourn Unit 3 and Unit 4 at 13:35 on 16-10-2025; likely related to a substation incident; exact cause unknown.

title: Some suggestions for the Nelson Review panel to consider (with respect to Forecasting) following review of the Draft Report
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-10 considering the draft Report of the Nelson Review
classified_content: The summary of the news "Some suggestions for the Nelson Review panel to consider (with respect to Forecasting) following review of the Draft Report" posted at 16-10-2025 08:48:00 AM is: This article provides detailed suggestions to the Nelson Review panel on improving electricity market forecasting in the Australian NEM, critiquing current AEMO models like the ESOO and MT PASA for issues such as unclear purpose, inclusion of uncommitted projects, exclusion of semi-scheduled units, and misaligned time horizons. The author recommends aligning medium-term instruments, requiring ESOO base case projects to appear in MT PASA, extending closure notice periods, redefining the ESOO as a true Statement of Commercial Opportunities, and using explicit closure dates for all assets to enhance reliability and investment signals. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: coal-fired policies. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Long-term. Its root_cause is: Inadequate forecasting methodologies and unclear market design objectives leading to potential reliability risks. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 16-10-2025. Its affected region is: Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants including generators, retailers, and consumers. Its cause type is: Regulatory and policy issues. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Unclear primary purpose of the ESOO; 2. Inclusion of anticipated projects in Base Case without proper tracking; 3. Exclusion of semi-scheduled units from MT PASA; 4. Inadequate treatment of closure dates for coal assets; 5. Misalignment of medium-term time horizons; 6. Lack of explicit closure dates for all assets in long-term modelling.

title: AEMO notes ‘Non-credible contingency event’ at Jeeralang
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO notes ‘Non-credible contingency event’ at Jeeralang" posted at 15-10-2025 09:15:00 AM is: On 15 October 2025, a non-credible contingency event occurred in Victoria’s power system when the JLTS No.2 220kV bus tripped at 0843 hrs, prompting AEMO to issue Market Notice MN129618. The cause was initially unknown, but AEMO confirmed no load shedding or disconnection of bulk electrical load occurred. By 0906 hrs, the bus was returned to service, and AEMO later identified the cause, deeming a recurrence unlikely under current circumstances, thus not reclassifying it as a credible contingency event. This event likely influenced AEMO’s earlier decision to rely on Murray instead of Jeeralang for system strength. Its relevance level is: Level 1. Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (within hours, resolved same day). Its root_cause is: Identified but not specified in the article; initially unknown. Its accident scale is: Minor – single bus trip with no load shedding or bulk load disconnection. Its dates is: 15-10-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria (VIC region), Australia. Its affected key users is: Unknown (no specific users mentioned, general grid). Its cause type is: Equipment failure (bus trip). Its causes is: [JLTS No.2 220kV bus tripped; cause later identified but not disclosed].

title: AEMO changes mind, and directs Murray instead … but this was also short-lived?!
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO changes mind, and directs Murray instead … but this was also short-lived?!" posted at 15-10-2025 09:05:00 AM is: On 15 October 2025, the Australian Energy Market Operator (AEMO) initially directed units at the Jeeralang Power Station in Victoria to address system strength issues, then cancelled those directions and instead directed two units at the Murray power station (MURR2-2 and MURR2-3) to operate in synchronous condenser mode, but these directions were also cancelled within an hour, leaving uncertainty about what measures were put in place to maintain system strength, especially with LYA4 reportedly offline. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: very short-term, from around 08:41 to 09:33 on 15 October 2025, with expected duration until 04:00 on 16 October 2025 but cancelled same day. Its root_cause is: need for system strength in the Victorian region, possibly exacerbated by the offline status of unit LYA4. Its accident scale is: Unknown. Its dates is: 15-10-2025. Its affected region is: Victoria, Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, Snowy Hydro Limited, and potentially all electricity market participants in Victoria. Its cause type is: operational intervention due to system strength deficiency. Its causes is: AEMO identified a system strength deficiency in Victoria, initially directing Jeeralang units, then switching to Murray units as synchronous condensers, then cancelling all directions – likely because the situation was recalculated or resolved.

title: AEMO issues directions to units at Jeeralang Power Station, for System Strength in Victoria (Wednesday 15th October 2025)

classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO issues directions to units at Jeeralang Power Station, for System Strength in Victoria (Wednesday 15th October 2025)" posted at 15-10-2025 08:22:00 AM is: AEMO issued directions to three generating units at EnergyAustralia's Jeeralang Power Station on 15 October 2025 to synchronise and follow dispatch targets to provide system strength in Victoria, but later cancelled these directions and instead directed the Murray unit to run as a synchronous condenser, all to maintain a secure operating state. Its root_cause is: AEMO identified a system strength deficiency in the Victorian region that required intervention to maintain power system security. title: An initial look at the Spot Price spike in NSW at 17:55 on Friday 10th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "An initial look at the Spot Price spike in NSW at 17:55 on Friday 10th October 2025" posted at 13-10-2025 09:12:00 AM is: On 10 October 2025 at 17:55, a short-lived price spike to the Market Price Cap ($20,300/MWh) occurred in the NSW region of the Australian National Electricity Market, driven by a combination of multiple coal unit outages (including VP5 due to turbine vibrations, ER03, ER04, and BW04 on planned outages), a transmission line outage on the Avon–Marulan 330kV line, and resulting network congestion that led to binding constraint equations and mispricing, with interconnector flows constrained and limited spare capacity. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 17:55 on 10 October 2025 (single dispatch interval). Its root_cause is: Combination of multiple coal unit outages (VP5 turbine vibration, ER03/ER04/BW04 planned outages), a transmission line outage (Avon–Marulan 330kV line), and resulting network congestion leading to binding constraints. Its accident scale is: 4 coal units offline (VP5, ER03, ER04, BW04) and one transmission line outage. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 10 October 2025. Its affected region is: NSW (New South Wales) region of the NEM. Its affected key users is: NSW electricity market participants (consumers and generators). Its cause type is: Technical (outages and network constraints) and operational (constrained dispatch). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1) VP5 turbine vibration causing outage; 2) ER03 and ER04 planned outages; 3) BW04 planned outage; 4) Avon–Marulan 330kV line outage; 5) Interconnector constraints (QNI and VIC1-NSW1); 6) Binding constraint equations causing mispricing and local price adjustments.

title: Two bouts of >2 hours of Non-Conformance at Aldoga Solar Farm on Sunday 12th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Two bouts of >2 hours of Non-Conformance at Aldoga Solar Farm on Sunday 12th October 2025" posted at 13-10-2025 08:14:00 AM is: On 12 October 2025, the Aldoga Solar Farm in Queensland experienced two periods of non-conformance, where the unit continued generating surplus power despite economic curtailment due to negative spot prices, causing a sustained dispatch error of up to 337 MW in the first bout and 1 MW in the second, leading to AEMO issuing market notices and invoking constraints to manage frequency impacts. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: turbine/generator failures (and grid/system instability). Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: 12 October 2025, during the two non-conformance periods (09:50–12:30 and 14:05–16:20). Its root_cause is: Economic curtailment triggered by a negative spot price (-$11.18/MWh) dropping below the unit's bid price (-$11.10), but the solar farm continued generating, resulting in a large positive supply-demand imbalance. Its accident scale is: First bout: 337 MW sustained non-conformance over ~2 hours 15 minutes; second bout: 1 MW non-conformance over ~2 hours 15 minutes. Its dates are: 12-10-2025 (same as main date). Its affected region is: QLD1 (Queensland region of the National Electricity Market). Its affected key users are: AEMO (market operator), other generators providing frequency control and regulation FCAS, and grid operators managing frequency stability. Its cause type is: Operational deviation (non-conformance under National Electricity Rules clause 3.8.23). Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) are: 1) Spot price dropped below the unit's bid price, triggering economic curtailment; 2) The unit failed to reduce output to the dispatched target of 0 MW, generating 143–337 MW surplus; 3) The first deviation lasted 6 dispatch intervals before a constraint was invoked; 4) A second, smaller deviation occurred later in the day.

title: Three themes from NEMDEV 2025
author: Dan Lee
classified_content: The summary of the news "Three themes from NEMDEV 2025" posted at 10-10-2025 12:26:00 PM is: The article covers three key themes from the NEMDEV 2025 conference: the critical but increasingly strained role of gas in managing winter peaks due to declining production and pipeline constraints, rising costs for wind and transmission projects threatening the energy transition, and emerging operational challenges from renewable variability and frequency management. It emphasizes that gas remains essential for firming until long-duration storage matures, but supply issues—especially in Victoria—and cost pressures are mounting, while reforms struggle to keep pace with operational realities.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: fuel supply issues (gas).; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Medium-term (ongoing and intensifying through the 2030s).; Its root_cause is: The structural challenge of decarbonising the grid while relying on gas for firming, compounded by declining domestic gas production, pipeline constraints, and rising costs for renewables and transmission infrastructure.; Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident reported).; Its dates is: 10-10-2025.; Its affected region is: Australia’s National Electricity Market (NEM), particularly the southern half (Victoria, South Australia, and New South Wales).; Its affected key users is: Electricity consumers, generators (especially gas-fired and renewable), network operators, and market regulators.; Its cause type is: Economic, regulatory, and supply chain factors.; Its causes is: 1. Declining conventional gas production in Victoria by the 2030s; 2. Pipeline constraints limiting gas flows from Queensland; 3. Rising wind project costs due to inflation, interest rates, and turbine supply costs; 4. Soaring transmission costs (from ~$3-4M/km to ~$10M/km); 5. Increased off-target deviations from semi-scheduled wind and solar farms affecting frequency management; 6. Political and economic barriers to new gas supply development.

title: What happens, when Supply ≠ Demand?
author: Paul McArdle
topic: 2025-10 considering the draft Report of the Nelson Review
classified_content: The summary of the news "What happens, when Supply ≠ Demand?" posted at 10-10-2025 08:00:00 AM is: The article provides a technical and economic exploration of supply-demand imbalance in the National Electricity Market (NEM), focusing on system frequency disturbances (instantaneous) and dispatch-interval economic inefficiencies. It references the draft Nelson Review report, highlighting how changes in plant mix (coal unit closures, VRE additions) and market rules (FFR, FPP) affect frequency performance. Using examples like the 19 August 2025 event and Bayswater unit trip, it explains the cost implications of oversupply and undersupply, and emphasizes the need to understand various imbalance event types for market efficiency. Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: Grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Ongoing, with immediate effects on system frequency and economic dispatch intervals, and specific events on 19 August 2025 and earlier dates. Its root_cause is: Imbalance between electricity supply and demand due to changes in plant mix (reduced inertia, VRE additions), forecasting inaccuracies, unit trips, and market rule changes. Its accident scale is: Not an accident; conceptual explanation with illustrative incidents. Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 10-10-2025 (main article date) with references to 19 August 2025, 23 March 2017, and 6 November 2020. Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia. Its affected key users is: AEMO, scheduled and semi-scheduled generators, consumers, and grid operators. Its cause type is: Operational and structural. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Reduction in system inertia from progressive closure of coal units; addition of variable renewable energy (VRE) under semi-scheduled arrangements; forecasting errors by AEMO; unit trips (e.g., Bayswater unit 3); and introduction of market rules such as Fast Frequency Response (FFR) and Frequency Performance Payments (FPP).

title: Why Gladstone’s prospective closure reveals a new kind of NEM failure
author: Greg Elkins
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Why Gladstone’s prospective closure reveals a new kind of NEM failure" posted at 09-10-2025 09:52:00 AM is: Rio Tinto's announcement that Gladstone Power Station (1,680 MW) could close by 2029, six years early, highlights a structural failure in the National Electricity Market (NEM) beyond typical coal-to-renewables transition issues. The article argues that distorted market signals, misplaced risk allocation, interstate knock-on effects (notably Eraring's extension in NSW suppressing Queensland coal revenues), and delays in system-strength investments (e.g., synchronous condensers stuck in RIT-T processes) are driving an uncoordinated exit. With Gladstone already operating at minimum load, the closure threatens system reliability, especially as Queensland's Energy Roadmap projects are not fully operational until after the critical 2028-29 pinch point. The failure stems from a mismatch where governments bear political accountability for blackouts but lack levers to manage orderly exits, while complex ownership and cross-border interventions create chaotic outcomes.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant). Its category is: grid/system instability. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: from 2025 to 2029 and potentially beyond, with critical reliability risks in 2028-2029. Its root_cause is: NEM market design flaws leading to misaligned incentives, delayed system strength investments (RIT-T process), and lack of coordinated planning, exacerbated by interstate interventions and complex ownership structures. Its accident scale is: potential closure of Queensland's largest coal plant (1,680 MW), already effectively mothballed. Its dates (if different from the main date) is: 09-10-2025 (same as news date). Its affected region is: Queensland (Gladstone) and interconnected regions in the NEM, particularly New South Wales due to system strength dependencies. Its affected key users is: Boyne aluminium smelter, Queensland electricity consumers, and all NEM participants reliant on system stability. Its cause type is: Market and regulatory failure. Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: 1. Rio Tinto's early closure announcement linked to Boyne contract expiry in 2029; 2. Eraring's extension in NSW suppressing Gladstone's economic case; 3. RIT-T process delays for synchronous condensers in NSW; 4. Complex ownership (Rio Tinto, NRG, Japanese investors) blurring accountability; 5. Lack of interstate planning coordination and inadequate regulatory response to system strength needs.

title: Aggregate (estimated) curtailment of Large Solar exceeds 70% on Sunday 5th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Aggregate (estimated) curtailment of Large Solar exceeds 70% on Sunday 5th October 2025" posted at 06-10-2025 11:51:00 AM is: On Sunday 5th October 2025, aggregate curtailment of Large Solar across the NEM peaked at 73% at 13:05 NEM time, with only 2,159 MW supplied out of 8,304 MW potential output; the weekend also saw curtailment above 60% on Saturday and a notable collective underperformance on Thursday 2nd October that contributed to frequency deviations, highlighting grid integration challenges.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant).; Its category is: grid/system instability.; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Immediate (during the weekend of 4-5 October 2025, with ongoing effects from preceding days).; Its root_cause is: High solar generation combined with network constraints and economic offloading leading to oversupply curtailment.; Its accident scale is: 73% curtailment (6,145 MW spilled) at peak, with sustained >60% curtailment over the weekend.; Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 02-10-2025 to 05-10-2025 (events spanning Thursday 2nd October to Sunday 5th October 2025).; Its affected region is: National Electricity Market (NEM), Australia.; Its affected key users is: Semi-Scheduled Solar Farms, grid operators (AEMO), and market participants.; Its cause type is: Operational curtailment (network and economic).; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: Network curtailment due to grid constraints; Economic offloading due to oversupply and negative pricing; Collective underperformance (positive Dispatch Error) on Thursday 2nd October causing frequency dips; Rapid output fluctuations on Friday 3rd October.

title: Also difficult to see any fluctuation in Mainland Frequency on Sunday morning 5th October 2025, with BW02 offline (two-shifting)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Also difficult to see any fluctuation in Mainland Frequency on Sunday morning 5th October 2025, with BW02 offline (two-shifting)" posted at 05-10-2025 03:25:00 PM is: On Sunday 5th October 2025, the BW02 generator unit was taken offline by 08:11 NEM time as part of a two-shifting operation, yet the Mainland Frequency remained remarkably stable with no observable downward fluctuation in the minutes prior to the outage, a situation similar to the previous day; this indicates that the unit's absence had negligible impact on grid frequency.  
Its relevance level is: Level 1.  
Its category is: turbine/generator failures.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term (single day, 5 October 2025).  
Its root_cause is: Scheduled two-shifting operation taking BW02 offline.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown (no accident occurred; planned outage).  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-10-2025.  
Its affected region is: Mainland (NEM – National Electricity Market, Australia).  
Its affected key users is: General electricity consumers on the mainland grid.  
Its cause type is: Operational (scheduled two-shifting).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: BW02 unit was taken offline for two-shifting operation; no instability or frequency fluctuation resulted.

title: Difficult to see any fluctuation in Mainland Frequency on Saturday morning 4th October 2025, with BW02 offline (two-shifting)
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "Difficult to see any fluctuation in Mainland Frequency on Saturday morning 4th October 2025, with BW02 offline (two-shifting)" posted at 05-10-2025 03:13:00 PM is: On 4th October 2025, the BW02 unit was offline from 08:06 to 15:06 NEM time due to two-shifting operation, but high-speed monitoring in Brisbane showed no noticeable impact on system frequency.; Its relevance level is: Level 2. Its category is: Grid frequency issues. Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short-term, during the offline period on 4th October 2025 (08:06 to 15:06 NEM time), with no observed market impact. Its root_cause is: Planned two-shifting operation of BW02 unit. Its accident scale is: None. Its dates is: 04-10-2025 and 05-10-2025. Its affected region is: NEM (Queensland region). Its affected key users is: None. Its cause type is: Operational (two-shifting). Its causes is: BW02 unit taken offline on Saturday 4th October 2025 from 08:06 to 15:06 NEM time for two-shifting.

title: Talk about two-shifting … such as with BW02 on Saturday 4th and Sunday 5th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content:
The summary of the news "Talk about two-shifting … such as with BW02 on Saturday 4th and Sunday 5th October 2025" posted at 05-10-2025 02:41:00 PM is: The article discusses AGL's two‑shifting of Bayswater Unit 2 (BW02) on 4–5 October 2025, a strategy where the boiler remains fired but the turbine is briefly offline to avoid low or negative prices and create midday headroom for renewables. It also notes a coincident AEMO direction for system strength in NSW on 5 October, which was cancelled shortly after BW02 went offline, and cautions observers against misidentifying similar events as two‑shifting without verifying rebid reasons.  
Its relevance level is: Level 2.  
Its category is: market reactions.  
Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Short‑term (hours on 4–5 October 2025, with previous occurrences in spring and autumn 2025).  
Its root_cause is: AGL’s operational decision to two‑shift BW02 as a cost‑effective alternative to running through very low or negative wholesale prices, while also supporting renewable integration.  
Its accident scale is: Unknown.  
Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 4–5 October 2025 (and earlier instances on 27 September 2025, 17/22/26 March/April 2025, etc.).  
Its affected region is: New South Wales, Australia (Bayswater power station).  
Its affected key users is: Electricity market participants (generators, retailers, AEMO).  
Its cause type is: Operational strategy (intentional two‑shifting).  
Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is:  
- AGL successfully trialled two‑shifting on Bayswater Unit 4 (7 September 2024).  
- Repeat two‑shifting on BW02 occurred in autumn 2025 (17, 22 March; 26 April) and spring 2025 (27 September; 4–5 October).  
- On 5 October 2025, an AEMO direction for system strength was issued at 07:25 and cancelled by 08:30, coinciding with BW02 going offline at 08:11.

title: AEMO issues (short-notice, short-lived) direction for System Strength in NSW on Sunday 5th October 2025
author: Paul McArdle
classified_content: The summary of the news "AEMO issues (short-notice, short-lived) direction for System Strength in NSW on Sunday 5th October 2025" posted at 05-10-2025 08:52:00 AM is: On 5 October 2025, AEMO issued a series of market notices for a short-notice intervention in the NSW region due to a system strength deficiency, directing Snowy Hydro Limited’s MURR2-2 MURRAY2 unit to synchronise and operate as a synchronous condenser from 07:30 to 08:30 hrs. The direction was issued at 07:20, declared an intervention event with no intervention pricing, and was cancelled shortly after at 08:30, resolving the system strength issue without escalation.; Its relevance level is: Level 1 (Most Relevant); Its category is: grid/system instability; Its Timeframe of the event impact on the electricity_market is: Very short-term, from the 07:25 dispatch interval to 08:30 hrs on 05-10-2025; Its root_cause is: Insufficient system strength in the NSW region due to a lack of adequate market response to maintain secure power system operation; Its accident scale is: Minor (short-lived directional intervention, no damage or extended outage); Its dates (if different from the main date; if the same, repeat the same date) is: 05-10-2025; Its affected region is: New South Wales (NSW); Its affected key users is: AEMO (operator), Snowy Hydro Limited (directed participant), and NEM market participants; Its cause type is: Operational/Grid management issue; Its causes (a summarized list of cause events) is: AEMO identified a foreseeable system strength deficiency in NSW requiring intervention; no sufficient market response was received by the 07:20 deadline; AEMO issued a direction to a synchronous condenser to provide system strength; the system returned to a secure state within one hour.
